region.name: Southland & Fiordland
region.description: The deep south — Fiordland's drowned valleys, the Catlins' wild coast, Stewart Island / Rakiura, and the wide sheep-country plains of Southland.

pois.name: Milford Sound / Piopiotahi
pois.short: Kipling called it 'the eighth wonder of the world' — a 15 km fiord walled by 1,200 m cliffs and crowned by Mitre Peak.
pois.commentary: Milford Sound — Piopiotahi — is the most-visited of Fiordland's 14 fiords and the one everyone photographs. Carved by Ice-Age glaciers to depths below sea level, the fiord is 15 km long and walled by cliffs that rise a vertical 1,200 m straight from saltwater to peak. Mitre Peak (1,692 m) is the defining pyramid. The name Piopiotahi — 'a single piopio' — comes from a Māori tradition of a lone thrush mourning a dead hero. Annual rainfall is ~7,000 mm, which means most days have waterfalls everywhere; on dry days the fiord is quieter. Two-hour cruise boats run from the head of the fiord; early morning or late afternoon have the best light and fewest coaches.

reviews.summary: Milford Sound / Piopiotahi draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Kipling called it 'the eighth wonder of the world' — a 15 km fiord walled by 1,200 m cliffs and crowned by Mitre Peak. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound / Patea
pois.short: Three times longer than Milford and ten times quieter — reached by a boat, a bus, and another boat.
pois.commentary: Doubtful Sound — Patea — is Fiordland's second-most-visited fiord and the one the Cook-era navigators actually named: 'Doubtful Harbour' in 1770, because Cook was doubtful whether the *Endeavour* could sail back out on the prevailing wind. It's 40 km long (three times Milford's length), has three distinct arms, and is noticeably less crowded. Access takes effort: drive to Manapōuri, 45-minute boat across Lake Manapōuri, 22 km coach over Wilmot Pass, then board the fiord vessel. Day cruises (7–8 hrs door-to-door) or overnight cruises with Real Journeys/Go Orange. Resident bottlenose dolphins, tawaki/Fiordland crested penguins, NZ fur seals.

reviews.summary: Doubtful Sound / Patea draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Three times longer than Milford and ten times quieter — reached by a boat, a bus, and another boat. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Te Anau
pois.short: The gateway to Fiordland — on the shore of New Zealand's second-largest lake, with its own glow-worm caves.
pois.commentary: Te Anau sits on the eastern shore of 344 km² Lake Te Anau, the country's second-largest lake (Taupō is larger) and its deepest (417 m). The town is Fiordland's service base — petrol, coaches to Milford, the only supermarket for 200 km. The Te Anau Glow-worm Caves — on the far side of the lake, reached by boat — are living, still-being-formed limestone caves whose ceilings are dotted with thousands of glow-worm larvae (*Arachnocampa luminosa*). Guided boat-and-walk tour, 2.25 hours. Also in town: the DOC Great Walks Centre (Kepler starts here); the endangered takahē bird live at the nearby wildlife park. Don't rush through.

reviews.summary: Te Anau draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The gateway to Fiordland — on the shore of New Zealand's second-largest lake, with its own glow-worm caves. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Manapōuri & West Arm Power Station
pois.short: The lake that saved New Zealand environmentalism — and the country's largest hydro station, hidden deep underground.
pois.commentary: Lake Manapōuri is the second-deepest lake in NZ (444 m) and the site of the country's most important environmental victory. In 1969 the government proposed raising Manapōuri 30 m to boost power generation for the Tiwai Point aluminium smelter. A petition — 265,000 signatures, 10% of the population — forced the 1972 Labour government to guarantee the lake would not be raised. The Save Manapōuri Campaign effectively founded the modern NZ environmental movement. The power station was still built — but underground: turbine hall hollowed out of 200 m of granite, water discharged via a 10 km tunnel to Doubtful Sound. Guided tours from West Arm during summer, ~4 hours from Manapōuri.

reviews.summary: Manapōuri & West Arm Power Station draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The lake that saved New Zealand environmentalism — and the country's largest hydro station, hidden deep underground. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Homer Tunnel
pois.short: A 1.2 km single-lane tunnel blasted through alpine rock — Depression-era pick-and-shovel work, now a kea hangout.
pois.commentary: The Homer Tunnel connects the Hollyford Valley to the Milford side of the Divide. Surveyed in 1889, begun in 1935 as Depression-era relief work — five men with picks and shovels, winter-isolated at 945 m, working by carbide lamp. Broken through in 1940, it didn't open to traffic until 1953. The tunnel is 1,207 m long on a 1:10 grade, and still mostly single-lane in each direction controlled by traffic lights (November–May). The eastern portal is classic kea territory — the olive-green alpine parrot will strip rubber seals off car windows given the chance. Avalanche paths above the tunnel close the road unpredictably every winter.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Homer Tunnel — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A 1. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: The Chasm
pois.short: A 20-minute loop walk through rainforest to a deep, sculpted waterfall pothole.
pois.commentary: The Chasm is a roadside stop on State Highway 94 about 10 km before Milford Sound. A 20-minute loop through silver beech and moss-covered rainforest leads to a series of bridges across the Cleddau River, which has carved a network of plunge pools and potholes into the granite. The rock is sculpted into abstract shapes by centuries of water and gravel working round and round; the main cascade is ~22 m. One of the best short walks in Fiordland and a perfect leg-stretcher between Te Anau and Milford. Stop on the way into Milford, not on the way out, when light is better.

reviews.summary: The Chasm draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 20-minute loop walk through rainforest to a deep, sculpted waterfall pothole. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Bluff / Stirling Point
pois.short: The bottom of State Highway 1 — marked by a yellow signpost and an oyster season the country stops for.
pois.commentary: Bluff — Awarua in Māori — is the southern end of State Highway 1, 1,452 km from Cape Reinga. The Stirling Point signpost at the road's end (on a headland above Foveaux Strait) points to London, New York, the Equator, and the South Pole. It's an obligatory photo. Bluff is working port, ferry terminal to Stewart Island (one hour across), and above all an oyster town — the annual Bluff Oyster Season (March–August) is the national gastronomic calendar. The flat *Ostrea chilensis* dredged from Foveaux Strait is prized globally. Also: the Bluff Hill Lookout (265 m) has 360° views of the strait, the aluminium smelter at Tiwai, and Stewart Island in the distance.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Bluff / Stirling Point as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The bottom of State Highway 1 — marked by a yellow signpost and an oyster season the country stops for. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Stewart Island / Rakiura
pois.short: New Zealand's third island — 85% national park, home to wild kiwi that walk the beaches in daylight.
pois.commentary: Rakiura — 'the glowing skies' — is the third and southernmost of New Zealand's main islands, 30 km across Foveaux Strait from Bluff. Population ~400, concentrated in the single village of Oban / Halfmoon Bay. 85% of the island is Rakiura National Park (NZ's youngest, 2002). Rakiura is the easiest place in NZ to see wild kiwi — the southern brown kiwi (tokoeka) emerges in daylight on some beaches, because there are few predators. The 32 km Rakiura Track Great Walk (3 days) is the best introduction. Access via 1-hour ferry from Bluff, or 20-minute flight from Invercargill. Aurora Australis visible on clear winter nights — this is one of NZ's few reliable aurora-viewing spots.

reviews.summary: Stewart Island / Rakiura draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. New Zealand's third island — 85% national park, home to wild kiwi that walk the beaches in daylight. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Invercargill & E Hayes Motorworks
pois.short: The southernmost city in New Zealand — Scottish-laid streets, and Burt Munro's original 1920 Indian in a hardware shop.
pois.commentary: Invercargill was founded in 1856 and laid out by Scottish surveyor John Turnbull Thomson on a broad grid with the streets named after Scottish rivers (Dee, Tay, Esk). It's the southernmost city in the country, population ~55,000. The unexpected highlight is E Hayes & Sons — a working hardware store on Dee Street that also displays the *World's Fastest Indian*: Burt Munro's modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle, which he rode to a 183.586 mph land-speed record at Bonneville in 1967, aged 68. The Anthony Hopkins film (2005) made him famous worldwide. The Hayes display is free, in the middle of the paint aisle. Also: Queen's Park, Southland Museum's tuatarium (currently under redevelopment).

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Invercargill & E Hayes Motorworks — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The southernmost city in New Zealand — Scottish-laid streets, and Burt Munro's original 1920 Indian in a hardware shop. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Nugget Point / Tokatā
pois.short: An 1870 lighthouse on a narrow headland above a scatter of sea-stack 'nuggets' — and the Catlins' most iconic view.
pois.commentary: Nugget Point — Tokatā in Māori — is the most-photographed headland in the Catlins. A narrow grass ridge leads out to an 1870 cast-iron lighthouse, with a string of weathered sea-stacks (the 'nuggets') scattered in the ocean below. Cook named them in 1770 because from offshore they resembled lumps of gold. The 20-minute walk in has handrails and a 76 m drop either side. The ledges below host fur seals year-round, NZ sea lions sometimes, elephant seals occasionally, and a yellow-eyed penguin colony. Sunrise is the photograph. Nearby Roaring Bay has a penguin hide. Part of the Catlins coast — allow a full day for the Catlins loop.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Nugget Point / Tokatā — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. An 1870 lighthouse on a narrow headland above a scatter of sea-stack 'nuggets' — and the Catlins' most iconic view. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Curio Bay Petrified Forest
pois.short: A 180-million-year-old Jurassic forest, fossilised on the tide platform — plus penguins and Hector's dolphins.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay, on the southern Catlins coast, has one of the best-preserved Jurassic fossil forests in the world. 180 million years ago, when NZ was part of Gondwana, a kauri/podocarp forest was repeatedly buried by volcanic ash and silicified. The low-tide platform exposes stumps still standing where they grew, and fallen logs turned to stone. Walk on the rock at low tide to see them in situ. The same bay hosts yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) coming ashore at dusk — stand back 50 m. In adjoining Porpoise Bay, a resident pod of Hector's dolphins — the world's smallest dolphin, ~1.4 m — often comes close to shore. Stay at the campground above the cliffs.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Curio Bay Petrified Forest earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A 180-million-year-old Jurassic forest, fossilised on the tide platform — plus penguins and Hector's dolphins. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

pois.name: McLean Falls
pois.short: The tallest waterfall in the Catlins — a 22 m two-tier cascade, 40 minutes' walk through rimu forest.
pois.commentary: McLean Falls is the Catlins' star waterfall — a 22 m two-tier cascade on the Tautuku River. The 40-minute return walk climbs gently through rimu, beech, and ancient podocarp forest; the base of the upper fall is a mossy amphitheatre you can walk into (slippery in wet weather). The bush is a 1,000-year-old intact podocarp stand, protected within the Catlins Conservation Park. Pair with nearby Purakaunui Falls (a tiered fall photographed on the old 20c stamp), Cathedral Caves (accessible only at low tide), and the Florence Hill Lookout over Tautuku Beach. Allow a full day for the Catlins coast; this is the centrepiece of it.

reviews.summary: McLean Falls draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The tallest waterfall in the Catlins — a 22 m two-tier cascade, 40 minutes' walk through rimu forest. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Gore — Brown Trout & Country Music Capital
pois.short: Officially the 'Brown Trout Capital of the World' and the 'Country Music Capital of New Zealand' — a small Southland town with two very specific claims.
pois.commentary: Gore has both self-appointed titles framed on the town entrance. The Mataura River — which flows past Gore — produces among the largest wild brown trout in the world, and the 3 m chainsaw-carved brown-trout statue on Main Street commemorates it. The annual Gold Guitars Awards (May/June, running since 1973) is the country's biggest country-music event; every Queen's Birthday weekend, the town becomes a festival. Historical oddity: Gore was central to Southland's Prohibition-era moonshine trade through the 1920s; Hokonui Moonshine Museum tells that story. Also: the Eastern Southland Gallery, which holds one of NZ's best regional art collections. Pair with Mandeville Airfield heritage planes.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Gore — Brown Trout & Country Music Capital is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Officially the 'Brown Trout Capital of the World' and the 'Country Music Capital of New Zealand' — a small Southland town with two very specific claims. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima
pois.short: One of the oldest European settlements in the country — a 1836 whaling station on the Aparima estuary, now the 'Riviera of Southland'.
pois.commentary: Riverton / Aparima was established in 1836 by Scottish whaler John Howell — making it one of the earliest continuously-occupied European settlements in New Zealand, pre-dating the Treaty of Waitangi by four years. Howell married Kohikohi, the daughter of a Kāi Tahu chief, and the town has deep Māori-European heritage. The whaling station was on Riverton Rocks; the bay was ideal for beach-based right-whale hunting. Today it's a small seaside town — the locals call it the 'Riviera of Southland' for its unusually sheltered and sunny microclimate. Te Hikoi Southern Journey heritage museum tells the story; the 1843 Howell's Point cottage is the oldest continuously-occupied European building in Southland.

reviews.summary: Riverton / Aparima draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. One of the oldest European settlements in the country — a 1836 whaling station on the Aparima estuary, now the 'Riviera of Southland'. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Tuatapere & Hump Ridge Track
pois.short: The 'Sausage Capital of New Zealand' and the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track — the country's 11th Great Walk.
pois.commentary: Tuatapere is a one-street milling town on the Waiau River in Western Southland. Its self-appointed title — 'Sausage Capital of NZ' — dates from the mid-20th century when the town's butchers competed for the country's best sausage prize. The town is also the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track, a loop through coastal Waitutu Forest, over the subalpine Hump Ridge (1,000 m, exposed), and back along the Fiordland south coast. Features: two historic 1925 timber viaducts, the sub-alpine tops, sea-cliff views across Te Waewae Bay. Upgraded to Great Walk status in 2024 — the country's 11th and one of its newest. 3 days of walking.

reviews.summary: Tuatapere & Hump Ridge Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The 'Sausage Capital of New Zealand' and the start of the 61 km Hump Ridge Track — the country's 11th Great Walk. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Slope Point
pois.short: The southernmost point of the South Island mainland — a bleak cliff and a famous row of wind-sculpted macrocarpa trees.
pois.commentary: Slope Point, in the Catlins, is the southernmost tip of the South Island. Accessed by a 20 minute walk across farmland.
pois.audio_story: You have come to Slope Point, the southernmost tip of the South Island mainland, and you are about to understand why the southern trees grow sideways. The access track crosses a working sheep paddock, about twenty minutes each way, and is closed during spring lambing. At the end of the walk you reach a low clifftop above a grey Southern Ocean that continues, uninterrupted, all the way to Antarctica. There is nothing between you and the ice. A yellow sign on a post marks the point with the distances to the equator and to the South Pole. The wind here is the wind of the Roaring Forties, and it never really stops. On your way to the point you will pass the famously leaning row of macrocarpa trees, planted as a shelter belt decades ago, now bent almost flat to the ground by the constant prevailing wind. The trees are one of the most photographed sights in New Zealand, and the photograph does not quite prepare you for the reality of standing beside them. Bring a warm coat even on a summer day. This is the edge of a continent. Stand for a few minutes. Then turn around and go home.

reviews.summary: Slope Point draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. The southernmost point of the South Island mainland — a bleak cliff and a famous row of wind-sculpted macrocarpa trees. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
pois.short: A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster.
pois.commentary: Built 1884 after the 1881 wreck of the SS Tararua, in which 131 people died. The lighthouse area is also a popular sea lion haul-out.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Waipapa Point Lighthouse, a small white wooden tower on a low windswept headland at the south eastern corner of Southland. The lighthouse looks modest, almost apologetic. The reason it exists is anything but. On the twenty ninth of April eighteen eighty one, the steamship SS Tararua struck a reef just offshore here in the early hours of the morning. The ship broke up within a day. One hundred and thirty one people died, including women, children, and most of the crew. Only twenty survived. It remains the worst civilian maritime disaster in New Zealand's history. In the inquiry that followed, Waipapa Point was finally lit, and in eighteen eighty four the lighthouse you are looking at was built, one of the last remaining wooden lighthouses in the country. Today the keeper's cottage is gone, the light is automated, and the headland is a quiet sheep paddock. You may well find a sea lion sleeping on the short access path or on the rocks below. Give them a very wide berth, at least twenty metres, and never get between a sea lion and the sea. Stand at the lighthouse, look out at the reef, and consider the names on the memorial stone.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipapa Point Lighthouse — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A wooden 1884 lighthouse built after the Tararua wreck — New Zealand's worst civilian maritime disaster. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Kepler Track
pois.short: A 60km alpine loop from Te Anau — Fiordland's only Great Walk that begins and ends in the same town.
pois.commentary: The Kepler Track is a 60km Great Walk in Fiordland National Park. Three or four days. Starts and finishes near Te Anau.
pois.audio_story: You are about to walk the Kepler Track, one of the ten Great Walks of New Zealand, and unusually among them, a loop that starts and finishes in the same town. The track was purpose built in nineteen eighty eight to relieve pressure on the older Milford and Routeburn tracks, and is now one of the most popular multi day tramps in Fiordland. From the control gates on Lake Te Anau, the track climbs through silver beech forest up to the high bush line, then out onto a dramatic open alpine ridge that runs for hours above twelve hundred metres. From the ridge you can see down into the fiord country to the west and across Lake Te Anau to the rolling tussock country to the east. The high section, between Luxmore Hut and Iris Burn Hut, is the highlight of the walk. It is also the most weather exposed. Do not attempt it in marginal conditions. The rest of the loop drops back down through beech forest along the Iris Burn river and around the shore of the lake back to the start. Most walkers take four days. Book huts well in advance through Department of Conservation.

reviews.summary: Kepler Track draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 60km alpine loop from Te Anau — Fiordland's only Great Walk that begins and ends in the same town. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara
pois.short: A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds.
pois.commentary: 266-hectare predator-free sanctuary off Stewart Island. Home to South Island saddleback, yellowhead, weka, kiwi, and many other species.
pois.audio_story: You have caught the water taxi across Paterson Inlet from Stewart Island to Ulva Island, known to Māori as Te Wharawhara. Ulva is two hundred and sixty six hectares of ancient rimu, rātā and kāmahi forest, and it is, crucially, entirely free of rats, stoats, possums and cats. That small miracle has been achieved over decades of patient Department of Conservation work, and the result is one of the most extraordinary experiences in the country. Within minutes of stepping off the jetty, you will be surrounded by native birds that are rare or absent almost everywhere else. The South Island saddleback and the yellowhead, both nearly extinct a generation ago, are common here. Weka patter along the tracks, unconcerned by humans. Kākā screech in the canopy. Bellbirds and tūī fill the understory with song. There is a good chance of seeing a Stewart Island tokoeka kiwi in daylight, because the species here is partly diurnal. A network of well formed walking tracks loops across the island, usually taking three to four hours at a slow pace. Bring lunch. Take nothing but photographs. Brush your boots for any hidden seeds before you step ashore.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. A predator-free island in Paterson Inlet — a short water taxi from Stewart Island and alive with birds. Morning and dusk visits are praised as the most rewarding times.

pois.name: Queens Park, Invercargill
pois.short: Invercargill's 80ha central park — botanic gardens, free aviaries, a golf course, and the Southland Museum pyramid.
pois.commentary: Queens Park is Invercargill's central park, established 1869. Large aviary, rose gardens, sport facilities, and the nationally significant Southland Museum.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Queens Park, Invercargill's eighty hectare central park, and you are in possibly the most generous city park in the country. The park was laid out in eighteen sixty nine on what was then the northern edge of the town, and over the next century and a half it has grown into a patchwork of everything a city could want. There are rose gardens and a Japanese garden, a small aviary with native birds, a herb garden, a children's playground, a public swimming pool, several sports grounds, and a full eighteen hole golf course all on the same site. Entry to almost everything is free. At the southern edge of the park stands the distinctive white pyramid of the Southland Museum and Art Gallery, a building that for many years also housed one of the world's most successful tuatara breeding programmes. The museum has been closed for seismic work for some years, and its reopening is eagerly awaited. While you are here, walk to the east side of the park for a good stand of native bush and the small stream known as the Otepuni. It is a park for a full afternoon. Bring a picnic.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Queens Park, Invercargill is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. Invercargill's 80ha central park — botanic gardens, free aviaries, a golf course, and the Southland Museum pyramid. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Stirling Point, Bluff
pois.short: The southernmost point of State Highway 1 — and the famous yellow signpost. Gateway to Stewart Island.
pois.commentary: Stirling Point marks the end of SH1. The signpost lists distances to global cities. Ferry to Stewart Island sails from Bluff.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Stirling Point, Bluff, the southernmost point of State Highway One and one of the most southern drivable points of mainland New Zealand. The famous yellow signpost at the end of the carpark shows distances to the South Pole, London, New York, the equator, and a handful of other celebrated destinations. Bluff itself is one of the oldest continuously settled European sites in New Zealand. The first whalers established a shore station here in the eighteen twenties, before the Treaty of Waitangi was signed. Permanent European settlement dates from the eighteen thirties. Ngāi Tahu, and before them the Waitaha and Kāti Māmoe, have occupied the coast here for many centuries longer. The port today is best known for two things, the ferry service to Stewart Island that crosses the Foveaux Strait, and the Bluff oyster, Ostrea chilensis, which is dredged from the strait each winter and prized throughout the country for its distinctive flavour. The annual Bluff Oyster Festival in May draws thousands of visitors. Stand at the signpost, look out across the Foveaux Strait to the dim blue line of Stewart Island, and turn around. Everything behind you is further north.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Stirling Point, Bluff is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The southernmost point of State Highway 1 — and the famous yellow signpost. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima
pois.short: Arguably the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in New Zealand — a shore whaling station from 1836.
pois.commentary: Riverton was founded 1836 as a shore whaling station by Captain John Howell. Original Māori settlement Aparima predates European arrival by centuries.
pois.audio_story: You are at Riverton, known to Māori as Aparima, and arguably the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in New Zealand. Captain John Howell, an English whaling captain who had married a senior Ngāi Tahu woman named Kohikohi in the early eighteen thirties, established a shore whaling station here in eighteen thirty six, four years before the Treaty of Waitangi was signed. Howell''s station processed southern right whales and humpback whales caught in the Foveaux Strait through the middle decades of the nineteenth century. The marriage between Howell and Kohikohi was not unusual in the pre Treaty era, and their many descendants are now part of both the Howell family and local Ngāi Tahu whakapapa, a direct living link between the earliest European settlement and the iwi that preceded it. The town today is small, around two thousand people, with a busy estuary, some of the best surf beaches in Southland, a small heritage centre called Te Hikoi, and a particular pride in its long dual heritage. The annual Kai festival celebrates the traditional food harvest of Aparima, and the Riverton mud crabs are arguably better than the more famous Bluff oysters. Stop at Te Hikoi first to orient yourself.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Riverton / Aparima — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Arguably the oldest continuously occupied European settlement in New Zealand — a shore whaling station from 1836. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Tuhawaiki Memorial, Tuturau
pois.short: Where Ngāi Tahu under Tūhawaiki ended Te Puoho''s 1836 raid — the southernmost engagement of the Musket Wars.
pois.commentary: In 1836 the Ngāti Tama chief Te Puoho-o-te-Rangi led a raid deep into Southland. At Tuturau, Ngāi Tahu under Tūhawaiki defeated and killed him.
pois.audio_story: You are near Tuturau, a farming settlement in eastern Southland that was the scene in eighteen thirty six of one of the most remarkable acts of war in the southern half of New Zealand. In eighteen thirty five and thirty six the Ngāti Tama chief Te Puoho o te Rangi, operating from a base on the north shore of Cook Strait, led an ambitious raiding party all the way down the West Coast, through the Haast Pass, and into the eastern valleys of Otago and Southland, attacking Ngāi Tahu settlements as he went. His intention appears to have been to establish permanent Ngāti Tama settlement in southern Ngāi Tahu rohe. The raid reached Tuturau in the winter of eighteen thirty six. While Te Puoho rested his exhausted party here, the southern Ngāi Tahu chief Tūhawaiki gathered a counter attack. In the battle that followed at Tuturau, Te Puoho was killed and his war party was destroyed. The engagement ended the Musket Wars in the South Island, preserved Ngāi Tahu''s hold on Murihiku, and cemented Tūhawaiki as the most respected rangatira in the south. A memorial cairn on the roadside marks the battlefield. Stop and read the inscription. A quiet paddock that changed the political map of the South Island.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tuhawaiki Memorial, Tuturau as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Ngāi Tahu under Tūhawaiki ended Te Puoho''s 1836 raid — the southernmost engagement of the Musket Wars. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Invercargill — Scottish Southland
pois.short: The Scottish founded capital of Southland — wide streets, an 1888 water tower, and an enduringly independent civic culture.
pois.commentary: Invercargill founded 1857 as the principal town of a newly separated Southland Province. Named for Captain William Cargill of the Otago settlement.
pois.audio_story: You are in Invercargill, the southernmost city in New Zealand and the principal town of Southland. It was founded in eighteen fifty seven by Scottish settlers who had broken away from the Otago settlement in the north. The name Invercargill combines Inver, the mouth of a river in Gaelic, with Cargill, after Captain William Cargill, the founder of the Otago settlement. From the beginning the town was planned on a grander scale than many colonial settlements. The central streets are unusually wide, originally intended to allow for bullock teams to turn with a full load of wool. The distinctive landmark is the one hundred and twelve foot red brick water tower in Doon Street, completed in eighteen eighty nine, which still has its operational cast iron reservoir on top. Another is the beautiful Edwardian tile interior of the Lion Hotel, and the restored Victorian facade of the old Southland Museum and Art Gallery pyramid. Bill Richardson Transport World, just outside the centre, is an enormous private collection of vintage vehicles, said to be the largest private automotive collection in the southern hemisphere. The town still has its Scottish character, with strong Presbyterian civic traditions, generally reliable weather, and one of the friendliest local welcomes anywhere in the country.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Invercargill — Scottish Southland — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The Scottish founded capital of Southland — wide streets, an 1888 water tower, and an enduringly independent civic culture. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Mataura Paper Mill & Ngāi Tahu River Claims
pois.short: Where Ngāi Tahu fought to protect their river — and eventually won recognition that rivers have rights.
pois.commentary: The Mataura was a traditional Ngāi Tahu tuna and kanakana fishery. From the 1890s the paper mill heavily polluted the river. Restoration efforts since the 1980s.
pois.audio_story: You are passing Mataura, a small Southland town on the lower Mataura River. For centuries before European arrival, this river was one of the most productive freshwater fisheries in Ngāi Tahu rohe, famous in particular for kanakana, the pouched lamprey that runs up the river each spring, and for large fat tuna. The first flour mill was built on the river here in the eighteen fifties, and from eighteen ninety eight a substantial paper mill operated on the riverbank, processing gorse, rags and wood pulp into newsprint. For most of the twentieth century the mill discharged untreated waste directly into the river, destroying both the fishery and the wider ecosystem. Ngāi Tahu and local communities began sustained efforts to restore the river from the nineteen seventies onwards. The paper mill finally closed in two thousand, although an enormous stockpile of toxic aluminium dross it had accumulated proved a stubborn problem that was not fully cleaned up until twenty twenty two. The kanakana run, slowly recovering, is once again starting to look like its old self in good years. Stop at the river park in Mataura township. You are looking at a wound in the landscape that is still healing, and at a victory of persistent iwi advocacy for a fairer relationship with freshwater.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mataura Paper Mill & Ngāi Tahu River Claims as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Ngāi Tahu fought to protect their river — and eventually won recognition that rivers have rights. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Monkey Island / Te Puka o Takitimu, Orepuki
pois.short: The small tidal islet said to be the anchor stone of the Tākitimu waka — and a fine safe swimming spot.
pois.commentary: The Southland Māori name Te Puka o Takitimu recognises the islet as the anchor stone of the Tākitimu waka which wrecked on this coast.
pois.audio_story: You are at Monkey Island, a small lumpy tidal islet just offshore at Orepuki. The English name, probably applied in the eighteen sixties or seventies, may refer to a pre colonial windlass used for hauling boats up the beach, or may just be a bit of sailor wit. The Māori name, Te Puka o Takitimu, is far more interesting. In Ngāi Tahu tradition, the great ancestral waka Tākitimu was wrecked on this southern coast after an epic voyage around New Zealand. The body of the waka is said to have become the Takitimu Range, visible from the beach on a clear day. The anchor stone, the puka, is this islet. Every large rounded boulder, every spit of land, every inland mountain in Southland that looks broadly waka shaped has a story in Ngāi Tahu tradition about its place in the story of Tākitimu. At low tide the sandy flat between the beach and the islet is dry and safe, and you can walk around and climb the knoll in twenty minutes return. The beach itself stretches south for several kilometres in a gentle curve, and the water, although cold, is reasonably safe for swimming on calm days.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Monkey Island / Te Puka o Takitimu, Orepuki as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The small tidal islet said to be the anchor stone of the Tākitimu waka — and a fine safe swimming spot. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Lumsden — Railway Junction Heritage
pois.short: A former rail junction where the Kingston Flyer once ran — and where the rail culture of Southland is quietly preserved.
pois.commentary: Lumsden was a major rail junction 1880s–1970s. Steam locomotive Ja 1274 plinth-mounted; surviving railway buildings converted to museums.
pois.audio_story: You are at Lumsden, a small crossroads town in the Waimea Plains of northern Southland, and for nearly a hundred years one of the most important rail junctions in the lower South Island. From the eighteen eighties until the nineteen seventies Lumsden was the junction point of the Main South Line and the branches running north into the Mavora country and west to the Mossburn and Tuatapere districts. The junction at its peak supported a locomotive depot, a freight workshop, turning triangles, and substantial rail community housing. When the branch lines closed in the nineteen sixties and seventies, most of the infrastructure was pulled up and the town contracted. A determined local historical society has preserved several of the surviving rail era buildings, installed a plinth mounted Ja class steam locomotive on the station site, and runs a small railway heritage museum in the old station building. One of the pleasures of Lumsden today is that the original wide main street, wide enough for a full rake of wagons to turn, is still there. An hour will do it. The coffee at the old Lumsden Hotel is surprisingly good. Pair it with a drive up the Mavora Road into remote cattle country.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Lumsden — Railway Junction Heritage is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. A former rail junction where the Kingston Flyer once ran — and where the rail culture of Southland is quietly preserved. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
pois.short: Where the 1881 wreck of the Tararua killed 131 people — the worst civilian shipwreck in NZ waters.
pois.commentary: The SS Tararua wrecked on Waipapa reef in April 1881 with 131 lives lost. The 1884 lighthouse was the direct result.
pois.audio_story: You are at Waipapa Point, a dune headland on the Catlins coast east of Bluff, and at the small eighteen eighty four wooden lighthouse that stands on the headland. The lighthouse exists because on the night of the twenty ninth of April, eighteen eighty one, the passenger steamer SS Tararua ran directly onto Waipapa Reef in a storm. Over one hundred and thirty of the one hundred and fifty one passengers and crew on board drowned. It remains, in terms of civilian life, the worst shipwreck to have occurred in New Zealand waters. The public outcry at the loss led directly to the construction of the lighthouse here three years later, and to improvements in lighthouses and coastal signalling throughout the country. The lighthouse tower, built entirely from kauri timber prefabricated in Auckland and shipped down, is one of the few surviving wooden lighthouse towers in New Zealand. Sea lions, Phocarctos hookeri, frequently haul out on the beach below the headland and can be a hazard for walkers approaching through the dunes. Keep a minimum distance of twenty metres. The small memorial at the lighthouse carries the names of those lost on the Tararua. The wreck itself lies in around eight metres of water just offshore and is occasionally visited by divers.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Waipapa Point Lighthouse — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Where the 1881 wreck of the Tararua killed 131 people — the worst civilian shipwreck in NZ waters. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.commentary: In Māori cosmology, the South Island is Te Waka a Māui and Stewart Island is the anchor stone. Ngāi Tahu tītī harvesting continues today.

pois.commentary: Whenua Hou is one of the primary breeding sites for kākāpō. For Ngāi Tahu, the bird is a taonga species and their involvement in recovery is a deep cultural commitment.

reviews.summary: The wildlife encounters at Whenua Hou / Codfish Island earn near-universal five-star reviews — visitors say seeing New Zealand's unique native species up close is unforgettable. pois.short: Te Anau is the gateway to fiords carved by the gods — the lake was an important Ngāi Tahu travel route.
pois.commentary: Lake Te Anau was part of the network of routes used by Ngāi Tahu to reach the fiords and their pounamu resources. The name may refer to 'the cave of swirling water', perhaps the Te Anau Caves with their glowworms.

pois.name: Catlins Coastal Heritage Drive
pois.short: Drive the wild Catlins coast — waterfalls, petrified forests, sea lions, and the southernmost point of the South Island.
pois.commentary: The Catlins stretches along the southeastern coast from Balclutha to Invercargill, a sparsely populated area of native forest, wild coastline, and remarkable wildlife. Highlights include Purakaunui Falls, Cathedral Caves, Curio Bay's petrified Jurassic forest, and Slope Point, the southernmost tip of the South Island. New Zealand sea lions, yellow-eyed penguins, and Hector's dolphins are regularly seen.
pois.audio_story: You are driving into the Catlins, and this stretch of coast is one of New Zealand's best-kept secrets. Between Balclutha and Invercargill, the southeastern corner of the South Island remains wild, forested, and sparsely populated. The road winds through stands of podocarp forest to a succession of natural wonders: Purakaunui Falls, a picture-perfect three-tiered cascade; Cathedral Caves, sea-carved caverns you can walk through at low tide; and Curio Bay, where a 180-million-year-old petrified forest lies exposed on the wave platform, its ancient tree stumps preserved in stone. At Slope Point, you stand at the southernmost tip of the South Island, the wind blasting so hard that the trees grow permanently sideways. Keep your eyes on the beaches — New Zealand sea lions haul out on the sand, and at dusk, yellow-eyed penguins waddle ashore to nest in the bush.

reviews.summary: The Catlins Coastal Heritage Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the wild Catlins coast — waterfalls, petrified forests, sea lions, and the southernmost point of the South Island. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Hump Ridge Track
pois.short: Walk a 61 km loop from coast to alpine ridge, crossing the spectacular Percy Burn Viaduct.
pois.commentary: The Hump Ridge Track is a 61 km, 3-day loop in the Hump Ridge area west of Tuatapere. The track climbs from Te Waewae Bay coastline to alpine ridges at over 900 m, passing through native forest and crossing the Percy Burn Viaduct — one of the largest remaining wooden rail viaducts in the world. It became New Zealand's newest Great Walk in 2023.
pois.audio_story: You are starting the Hump Ridge Track at Rarakau on Te Waewae Bay, and over the next three days you will climb from wild southern coast to alpine ridge and back. The track became New Zealand's newest Great Walk in 2023, and it deserves the title. The first day follows the coast, where the Southern Ocean swells crash onto rocky platforms, before climbing steeply through podocarp forest to the first hut. The highlight of day two is the Percy Burn Viaduct — a massive wooden rail viaduct 36 metres high, built in 1930 for the timber industry and now one of the largest surviving wooden viaducts in the world. Walking across it, with the forest canopy at your feet and the valley falling away, is unforgettable. The alpine section of the ridge offers views south to Stewart Island and north to the Fiordland mountains.

reviews.summary: The Hump Ridge Track route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk a 61 km loop from coast to alpine ridge, crossing the spectacular Percy Burn Viaduct. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Oreti Beach Drive
pois.short: Drive along the vast Oreti Beach where Burt Munro tested his famous Indian motorcycle.
pois.commentary: Oreti Beach stretches 26 km along the southern coast west of Invercargill. It is one of New Zealand's few beaches where vehicles are permitted. Burt Munro, the subject of the film 'The World's Fastest Indian,' used Oreti Beach to test his modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle before setting world speed records at Bonneville. The beach is also a traditional toheroa shellfish gathering site.
pois.audio_story: You are driving onto Oreti Beach, and the sand stretches so far in both directions that the ends disappear into sea mist. This 26-kilometre beach is one of the few in New Zealand where you can drive a car on the sand, and it has a special place in Kiwi mythology. This is where Burt Munro — the Invercargill local immortalised in 'The World's Fastest Indian' — tested his heavily modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle, coaxing impossible speeds from an engine he rebuilt in his garden shed over 40 years. Munro went on to set world speed records at Bonneville that still stand today. The beach itself is wild and windswept, facing the Southern Ocean with nothing between you and Antarctica but grey sea. In the right conditions, surfers ride the break at the western end, and local Maori have gathered toheroa shellfish here for centuries.

reviews.summary: The Oreti Beach Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive along the vast Oreti Beach where Burt Munro tested his famous Indian motorcycle. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Rakiura Track (Stewart Island)
pois.short: Walk the Great Walk on New Zealand's third island — a 32 km loop through primeval bush and wild coast.
pois.commentary: The Rakiura Track is a 32 km, 3-day Great Walk loop on Stewart Island / Rakiura, starting and finishing in Oban. The track passes through dense podocarp and coastal forest, along sheltered shorelines, and through some of the most pristine bush remaining in New Zealand. Stewart Island has a population of about 400 people and is one of the best places to see kiwi in the wild.
pois.audio_story: You are stepping onto the Rakiura Track on Stewart Island, New Zealand's third island, and the bush here is as close to primeval as you will find anywhere in the country. The 32-kilometre loop takes three days and circles through dense podocarp forest — rimu, miro, and kamahi trees draped in mosses and ferns so thick the forest floor is permanently soft underfoot. Stewart Island has just 400 human residents but uncountable kiwi, and this is one of the very best places in New Zealand to see one in the wild. Join a guided evening walk on Ocean Beach: as darkness falls and your eyes adjust, the unmistakable silhouette of a kiwi probing the sand with its long bill appears — a moment that stays with you. The track also follows the coastline of Paterson Inlet, where the water is glass-calm and the only sounds are bellbirds and the lap of the tide.

reviews.summary: The Rakiura Track (Stewart Island) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk the Great Walk on New Zealand's third island — a 32 km loop through primeval bush and wild coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Southern Scenic Route
pois.short: A 610 km scenic route linking Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Invercargill, and the Catlins.
pois.commentary: The Southern Scenic Route covers approximately 610 km from Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Riverton, Invercargill, and the Catlins coast. It is one of New Zealand's designated scenic routes and passes through some of the least-visited parts of the South Island. Key stops include the old whaling town of Riverton (est. 1836), Colac Bay, and Fortrose.
pois.audio_story: You are driving the Southern Scenic Route, and this 610-kilometre road takes you through the deep south of New Zealand — a region most tourists miss entirely. From Queenstown, the route heads to Te Anau and then turns south through farmland to Riverton, one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand, established in 1836 as a shore whaling station. The road follows the southern coast through Colac Bay, where surfers ride breaks that arrive unimpeded from the Southern Ocean, and on through Invercargill with its broad, wind-swept streets. East of the city, the route enters the Catlins, where wild coastline, native forest, and wildlife replace the farmland. This is driving for people who want to see the real New Zealand — no adventure tourism, no boutique hotels, just land and sea and the occasional farmhouse smoke rising against a grey sky.

reviews.summary: The Southern Scenic Route route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A 610 km scenic route linking Queenstown to Dunedin via Te Anau, Invercargill, and the Catlins. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Lake Manapouri Cruise to West Arm
pois.short: Cruise across one of NZ's deepest lakes to the underground power station that sparked a conservation movement.
pois.commentary: Lake Manapouri is the second-deepest lake in New Zealand at 444 m. The cruise to West Arm provides access to the Manapouri Underground Power Station, built 200 m underground inside solid rock. The proposal to raise the lake level for the power scheme in the 1960s sparked New Zealand's first major conservation campaign, which became a defining moment in the country's environmental movement.
pois.audio_story: You are cruising across Lake Manapouri, and beneath the hull the water drops to 444 metres — this is one of the deepest lakes in New Zealand. The surface is mirror-calm on a still day, reflecting the Kepler Mountains and Cathedral Peaks with unsettling precision. At West Arm, you descend into the Manapouri Underground Power Station, carved 200 metres into solid rock. The turbine hall is vast and echoing, powered by water falling through tunnels to Doubtful Sound on the other side of the mountains. But this place has a significance beyond engineering: in the 1960s, a proposal to raise the lake level for the power scheme triggered New Zealand's first mass environmental petition. Nearly 265,000 people — ten percent of the entire population — signed it. The lake level was saved, and the modern conservation movement in New Zealand had found its voice.

reviews.summary: The Lake Manapouri Cruise to West Arm route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Cruise across one of NZ's deepest lakes to the underground power station that sparked a conservation movement. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima Heritage Walk
pois.short: Explore one of NZ's oldest European settlements, a whaling town where Maori and Pakeha history intertwine.
pois.commentary: Riverton / Aparima, established in 1836, is one of the earliest permanent European settlements in New Zealand. The town developed around shore whaling and sealing operations and had a notably mixed Maori-European community from its earliest days. Te Hikoi Museum tells the intertwined stories of tangata whenua and early settlers.
pois.audio_story: You are walking through Riverton, and this quiet seaside town at the mouth of the Aparima River holds a claim that few New Zealand settlements can match — it has been continuously inhabited by Europeans since 1836, making it one of the oldest Pakeha settlements in the country. But the story here is not just European. Riverton developed as a place where Maori and Pakeha lived and worked together from the beginning, intermarrying and trading in a way that was unusual for the era. The shore whalers who set up here needed Maori knowledge and cooperation, and the result was a community of mixed heritage. Te Hikoi Museum on the main street tells this story beautifully. Walk along the estuary where the whaling boats once launched and you will find a town that wears its age gracefully.

reviews.summary: The Riverton / Aparima Heritage Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Explore one of NZ's oldest European settlements, a whaling town where Maori and Pakeha history intertwine. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Te Anau to Manapouri Lakeshore Drive
pois.short: A short lakeshore drive between two Fiordland gateway towns with mountain views across the water.
pois.commentary: The 20 km road between Te Anau and Manapouri follows the shore of Lake Te Anau, New Zealand's second-largest lake, with views across the water to the Fiordland mountains. Te Anau is the gateway to Milford Sound and the Milford Track, while Manapouri is the departure point for Doubtful Sound cruises.
pois.audio_story: You are driving the short road between Te Anau and Manapouri, and do not rush this one. Lake Te Anau stretches to your left, the second-largest lake in New Zealand, its far shore backed by mountains that disappear into cloud. On a clear day, the peaks are reflected so perfectly in the lake that you cannot tell where the water ends and the sky begins. Te Anau itself is the gateway town for Fiordland — Milford Sound, the Milford Track, the Kepler Track all start from here. Manapouri, 20 kilometres south, is smaller and quieter, the departure point for Doubtful Sound. Between the two, this stretch of road gives you a gentle introduction to the scale of Fiordland, a national park so vast and wild that significant portions of it have never been explored on foot.

reviews.summary: The Te Anau to Manapouri Lakeshore Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A short lakeshore drive between two Fiordland gateway towns with mountain views across the water. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Bluff & Stirling Point Heritage Trail
pois.short: Stand at the bottom of SH1 at Stirling Point — where the road ends and the Southern Ocean begins.
pois.commentary: Bluff is the southern terminus of State Highway 1, which runs the full length of New Zealand from Cape Reinga. Stirling Point features the famous signpost showing distances to world cities. Bluff is renowned for its oysters (Bluff oysters are considered a national delicacy) and has been a port town since the 1820s. Bluff Hill / Motupohue offers panoramic views of Foveaux Strait and Stewart Island.
pois.audio_story: You are standing at Stirling Point in Bluff, and you have reached the bottom of State Highway 1. This road starts at Cape Reinga at the very top of the North Island and ends here, at a signpost pointing to the South Pole, London, New York, and everywhere else that is not here. Bluff is New Zealand's end of the road, a working port town that has sent ships into the Southern Ocean since the 1820s. The town is famous for one thing above all: oysters. Bluff oysters are dredged from the cold waters of Foveaux Strait between March and August, and Kiwis treat them with something approaching reverence. Climb Bluff Hill for a view that puts it all in perspective — Stewart Island floating on the southern horizon, Foveaux Strait churning between, and the sense that the next stop south is Antarctica.

reviews.summary: The Bluff & Stirling Point Heritage Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Stand at the bottom of SH1 at Stirling Point — where the road ends and the Southern Ocean begins. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Takitimu Mountains Drive
pois.short: Drive beneath the Takitimu Mountains, a remote range sacred to Ngai Tahu on the Fiordland fringe.
pois.commentary: The Takitimu Mountains are a remote range rising to 1,580 m between Te Anau and Riverton. The range is sacred to Ngai Tahu, who identify it as the upturned hull of the ancestral waka Takitimu. The back roads beneath the range pass through rolling farmland with few other vehicles, offering views of a mountain range that most visitors never see.
pois.audio_story: You are driving the back roads beneath the Takitimu Mountains, and chances are you have never heard of this range. Most visitors to Southland are focused on Fiordland to the west, but the Takitimu rises to 1,580 metres from the farmland east of Te Anau, dramatic and largely unvisited. For Ngai Tahu, these mountains are the upturned hull of the ancestral waka Takitimu, one of the great canoes that brought Maori to New Zealand. The ridge does indeed look like an overturned canoe from certain angles. The roads around the base are quiet — mostly gravel, mostly empty — and the farmland has a quality of deep, settled peace that feels rare in a world that moves fast. If you want to see Southland as locals know it, away from every tourist trail, this is where you drive.

reviews.summary: The Takitimu Mountains Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive beneath the Takitimu Mountains, a remote range sacred to Ngai Tahu on the Fiordland fringe. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Waiau River Scenic Road
pois.short: Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast.
pois.commentary: The road from Manapouri follows the Waiau River south through remote Southland farmland to Tuatapere. The Waiau drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri and is one of the most powerful rivers in Southland. The drive passes through a landscape of rolling green hills backed by the Takitimu Mountains and offers a glimpse of deep rural Southland.
pois.audio_story: You are driving south along the Waiau River from Manapouri, and the road here takes you through Southland as few tourists see it. The Waiau is a big river — it drains both Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri — and its green water moves with a steady, muscular current past farmland so quiet you can hear the birds from the car. The road follows the river valley between low hills, with the Takitimu Mountains occasionally appearing to the east. There are no towns for long stretches, just farms, sheep, and the kind of silence that takes getting used to. At Clifden, the historic suspension bridge — built in 1899 and now retired from vehicle use — is worth a stop. You can walk across it and feel it sway, imagining the horse-drawn carts that once crossed here when this was the main road south.

reviews.summary: The Waiau River Scenic Road route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive along the Waiau River from Lake Manapouri through remote farmland toward the coast. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Mirror Lakes Walk (Milford Road)
pois.short: A short boardwalk to lakes that perfectly reflect the Earl Mountains on still mornings.
pois.commentary: Mirror Lakes is a short walk off the Milford Road (SH94) to a series of small lakes that produce perfect reflections of the surrounding Earl Mountains. A boardwalk and viewing platform allow visitors to see the famous reflection. The sign at the lake is deliberately written backwards so it reads correctly in the reflection — a detail that delights photographers.
pois.audio_story: You are walking the boardwalk to the Mirror Lakes, and this short detour off the Milford Road is one of those quiet moments that stays with you. The lakes are small and perfectly still, sheltered from wind by the surrounding bush, and in their surface the Earl Mountains are reflected with a precision that makes you question which way is up. There is a sign at the viewing platform that appears to be written backwards — a deliberate trick, because when you look at its reflection in the water, the letters read perfectly. It is a small, clever joke that captures something about this place: nature does the heavy lifting here, and all you have to do is stand still and look. Come early in the morning before the breeze picks up, when the reflections are sharpest and the birdsong is loudest.

reviews.summary: The Mirror Lakes Walk (Milford Road) route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. A short boardwalk to lakes that perfectly reflect the Earl Mountains on still mornings. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Milford Sound Scenic Cruise
pois.short: Cruise beneath towering cliffs and waterfalls in the eighth wonder of the world.
pois.commentary: Milford Sound scenic cruises navigate the full length of the 16-kilometre fiord, passing beneath Mitre Peak (1,692m), through waterfall spray at Stirling Falls, and out to the Tasman Sea entrance. Multiple operators offer cruises ranging from two hours to overnight. Dolphins, seals, and penguins are frequently spotted.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Milford Sound, Piopiotahi, widely regarded as the most stunning natural attraction in New Zealand. As your cruise vessel glides down the 16-kilometre fiord, the scale of this place will take your breath away. Mitre Peak rises 1,692 metres directly from the dark water — that's five times the height of the Empire State Building. Waterfalls plunge from hanging valleys hundreds of metres above, and your captain will nose the boat right under Stirling Falls, close enough to feel the spray on your face. The fiord receives around seven metres of rainfall per year, and after heavy rain, temporary waterfalls cascade from every cliff face in their thousands. Watch the rocks at the water's edge for New Zealand fur seals, and keep your eyes on the surface for bottlenose dolphins. Rudyard Kipling wasn't exaggerating when he called this the eighth wonder of the world.

reviews.summary: The Milford Sound Scenic Cruise adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Cruise beneath towering cliffs and waterfalls in the eighth wonder of the world. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound Kayaking
pois.short: Paddle the vast, silent waters of Doubtful Sound — Fiordland's hidden gem.
pois.commentary: Doubtful Sound is three times longer and ten times larger than Milford Sound, yet receives a fraction of the visitors. Access requires a boat across Lake Manapouri and a bus over Wilmot Pass. Kayaking here offers encounters with bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins in profound silence.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Doubtful Sound, the Sound of Silence, and arguably the most remote and untouched place you can easily visit in New Zealand. Getting here is an adventure in itself — a boat across beautiful Lake Manapouri, then a bus over the dense bush of Wilmot Pass. But when you launch your kayak onto these dark, still waters, you'll understand why people make the journey. Doubtful Sound is three times longer than Milford Sound and ten times its size, yet you might be the only kayakers on the water. The silence is extraordinary — broken only by birdsong, the occasional splash of a dolphin, and the drip of your paddle. A resident pod of around 60 bottlenose dolphins lives here year-round, and Fiordland crested penguins — one of the world's rarest — nest along the shores. Captain Cook named it Doubtful Harbour in 1770 because he was doubtful he could sail out again. By kayak, you'll have no such concerns.

reviews.summary: The Doubtful Sound Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Paddle the vast, silent waters of Doubtful Sound — Fiordland's hidden gem. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Te Anau Glow Worm Caves
pois.short: Boat into ancient caves lit by thousands of glow-worms on Lake Te Anau.
pois.commentary: The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves are accessed by a short boat ride across Lake Te Anau. Rediscovered in 1948, the caves feature rushing underground streams and a grotto illuminated by thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa glow-worms. The cave system is geologically young, still being actively carved by water.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the shores of Lake Te Anau, where a boat is waiting to carry you across the lake to one of New Zealand's most magical underground experiences. The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves were rediscovered in 1948 by local explorer Lawson Burrows, though Maori knew of them centuries earlier. You'll walk through caverns carved by rushing underground streams, past whirlpools and waterfalls, deeper and deeper into the limestone. Then the lights go out, and you drift by boat into the glow-worm grotto. Thousands of tiny Arachnocampa luminosa larvae light up the cave ceiling like a constellation, their blue-green glow reflecting off the still water. The silence, the darkness, and the living lights create an atmosphere that is genuinely otherworldly. These caves are still geologically active — the water is still carving, still creating — making them a living, breathing wonder.

reviews.summary: The Te Anau Glow Worm Caves adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Boat into ancient caves lit by thousands of glow-worms on Lake Te Anau. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: The Chasm Walk - Milford Road
pois.short: Peer into a sculpted rock chasm where the Cleddau River thunders through carved stone.
pois.commentary: The Chasm is a short 20-minute return walk off the Milford Road where the Cleddau River has carved extraordinary shapes into solid rock over thousands of years. Bridges span the narrow gorge, allowing views directly down into swirling potholes and rock formations. Free access.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at The Chasm, a geological marvel just off the Milford Road. A short, easy walk through mossy beech forest brings you to a series of bridges spanning a narrow gorge where the Cleddau River has spent millennia carving extraordinary shapes into solid rock. Look down and you'll see polished potholes, swirling cauldrons, and smooth rock channels sculpted by the relentless power of water. The force of the river here is impressive — water thunders through gaps barely a metre wide, having carved its way through the rock with the patience of deep time. This is a free, easily accessible stop that many people rush past on their way to Milford Sound, but it's one of the most impressive geological features on the entire road. The 20-minute walk is worth every step.

reviews.summary: The The Chasm Walk - Milford Road adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Peer into a sculpted rock chasm where the Cleddau River thunders through carved stone. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Riverton Surfing
pois.short: Surf the powerful Southern Ocean swells at New Zealand's deep south beaches.
pois.commentary: Riverton and nearby Colac Bay offer consistent surf breaks powered by Southern Ocean swells. The beach breaks suit intermediate to experienced surfers, with occasional world-class conditions. A thick wetsuit is essential in these chilly waters. The small town of Riverton is one of New Zealand's oldest settlements.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the wild shores of Riverton, about as far south as surfing gets in New Zealand. The Southern Ocean delivers powerful, consistent swells that break along the sandy beaches of Riverton and nearby Colac Bay, producing waves that can rival anything further north. The water is cold — properly cold — so a good thick wetsuit is non-negotiable, but that's a small price for the waves and the solitude you'll find here. You might share the break with one or two locals, but crowd pressure is virtually unknown. The small town of Riverton, known as Aparima, is one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand, founded by whalers and sealers in the early 1800s. After your surf, the local fish and chips are legendary. This is raw, authentic, Southern Ocean surfing at the bottom of the world.

reviews.summary: The Riverton Surfing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Surf the powerful Southern Ocean swells at New Zealand's deep south beaches. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Rakiura / Stewart Island Nature Experience
pois.short: Spot wild kiwi on their beach at New Zealand's third island — Rakiura.
pois.commentary: Rakiura / Stewart Island is New Zealand's third largest island and one of the best places to see wild kiwi in their natural habitat. Guided evening walks on Ocean Beach offer reliable kiwi sightings. The island has 280 km of walking tracks, pristine beaches, and 85% of its land is national park.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Rakiura, Stewart Island — New Zealand's third and most remote island, where the native bush is so dense and the human population so small that the kiwi outnumber the people. This is one of the very few places in New Zealand where you can reliably see wild kiwi in their natural habitat, feeding on the beaches at dusk. Guided walks to Ocean Beach time the visit for when the Southern Tokoeka kiwi emerge from the bush to forage in the sand. Watching a wild kiwi probe the beach with its long bill, utterly unconcerned by your presence, is one of the most special wildlife experiences in the country. Eighty-five percent of the island is national park, and its 280 kilometres of walking tracks explore ancient forests, deserted beaches, and coastline that faces nothing but the Southern Ocean all the way to Antarctica.

reviews.summary: The Rakiura / Stewart Island Nature Experience adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Spot wild kiwi on their beach at New Zealand's third island — Rakiura. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Waiau River Jet Boat - Fiordland
pois.short: Jet boat the Waiau River through pristine Fiordland wilderness.
pois.commentary: Jet boat tours on the upper Waiau River between Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapouri explore one of the most scenic river stretches in Fiordland. The short river connects the two major lakes and is flanked by dense native bush and mountain scenery. The calm sections contrast with thrilling high-speed manoeuvres.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the banks of the upper Waiau River, a short but stunning stretch of water connecting Lake Te Anau to Lake Manapouri in the heart of Fiordland. Your jet boat will race along this pristine river, flanked by towering native bush and mountain scenery that has changed little in thousands of years. The Waiau is one of the most photogenic rivers in the South Island, its clear waters reflecting the beech forest and peaks above. Between the adrenaline of high-speed runs and 360-degree spins, your driver will slow down to point out native birdlife and share stories of the region's history. This river was once a vital travel route for Maori seeking pounamu on the West Coast. Today it's your express lane into the wilderness of Fiordland.

reviews.summary: The Waiau River Jet Boat - Fiordland adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Jet boat the Waiau River through pristine Fiordland wilderness. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Fiordland Horse Trekking
pois.short: Ride horses through Fiordland farmland with mountain and lake views.
pois.commentary: Horse trekking near Te Anau takes riders through high-country farmland and native bush with views of Lake Te Anau and the Fiordland mountains. Rides suit all experience levels, from gentle walks to longer backcountry treks. The area is home to working sheep and deer stations.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the base for horse trekking in the Fiordland region, where working farmland meets true wilderness. Your horse will carry you through open tussock pastures with views across Lake Te Anau to the snow-capped peaks of Fiordland National Park. The high-country stations around Te Anau run sheep and deer across some of the most beautiful farmland in New Zealand, and riding through it gives you a genuine connection to the rural character of the deep south. The horses are calm and well-trained, so beginners are welcome, while more experienced riders can join longer treks that venture into native bush and river flats. The light in Fiordland has a quality all its own — clear, golden, and somehow deeper than anywhere else. On horseback, moving at the landscape's own pace, you absorb it all.

reviews.summary: The Fiordland Horse Trekking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride horses through Fiordland farmland with mountain and lake views. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Bluff Oyster & Fishing Experience
pois.short: Fish the southern seas and taste world-famous Bluff oysters at NZ's southernmost town.
pois.commentary: Bluff is New Zealand's southernmost town on the mainland and home to the legendary Bluff oyster (Tiostrea chilensis). Fishing charters from Bluff explore Foveaux Strait, known for blue cod, paua, and crayfish. The Bluff oyster season runs from March to August, with the annual Bluff Oyster Festival in May.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Bluff, the southernmost town on mainland New Zealand, where the signpost at Stirling Point marks the distance to everywhere and the start of nowhere. Bluff is famous for one thing above all else — its oysters. The Bluff oyster is harvested from the wild beds of Foveaux Strait and is considered by many to be the finest oyster in the world, with a rich, creamy, intensely savoury flavour that has inspired a cult following. A fishing charter from Bluff takes you out into the strait, where you can fish for blue cod, try your hand at crayfish potting, and if you're here in season, taste freshly dredged oysters on the boat. The strait is wild water — it funnels the full force of the Southern Ocean — and the experience of being out here, with Stewart Island visible across the channel, is raw and exhilarating. This is the edge of New Zealand, and it tastes incredible.

reviews.summary: The Bluff Oyster & Fishing Experience adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Fish the southern seas and taste world-famous Bluff oysters at NZ's southernmost town. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Milford Sound Scuba Diving
pois.short: Dive Milford Sound's unique freshwater layer to discover deep-water species in the shallows.
pois.commentary: Milford Sound diving is unique due to a permanent layer of tannin-stained freshwater sitting atop the saltwater, blocking sunlight and allowing deep-water species like black coral to grow at remarkably shallow depths.
