Guided dives explore cliff walls encrusted with rare black and red corals, anemones, and unusual fish species.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the most extraordinary dive sites in the world. Milford Sound receives an average of seven metres of rainfall per year, creating a permanent layer of dark, tannin-stained freshwater that sits on top of the saltwater. This freshwater layer blocks sunlight, tricking deep-water marine organisms into growing at incredibly shallow depths. The result is a diver's wonderland — black coral trees that normally grow at 100 metres depth thrive here at just 15 metres. The cliff walls are encrusted with delicate red coral, jewel anemones, brachiopods, and other creatures that most divers only ever see on deep technical dives. As you descend through the freshwater layer into the clear saltwater below, it's like passing through a curtain into another world. Milford Sound's underwater environment has been compared to diving in the deep sea without the depth. It's genuinely unique — there is nowhere else on earth quite like this.

reviews.summary: The Milford Sound Scuba Diving adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dive Milford Sound's unique freshwater layer to discover deep-water species in the shallows. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Oyster Cove Restaurant & Bar
pois.short: Bluff's waterfront seafood restaurant — fresh Bluff oysters with 180-degree views to Stewart Island.
pois.commentary: Oyster Cove sits at Stirling Point in Bluff with spectacular views across to Stewart Island. During oyster season (March to August), this is the pilgrimage destination for lovers of Bluff oysters — big, fat and briny from the clear Foveaux Strait waters. The seafood platter (oysters, blue cod, mussels, smoked salmon) showcases the best of southern seafood. Outside oyster season, the blue cod and mussels carry the menu.

reviews.summary: The Bluff oysters in season are world-class. The Stewart Island views add drama. Some note limited options outside oyster season.

pois.name: Barnes Wild Bluff Oysters
pois.short: The legendary Invercargill oyster shed — queues out the door for fresh Bluff oysters and fish 'n' chips.
pois.commentary: Barnes is a small iron shed on Spey Street in Invercargill that has achieved near-mythical status. During oyster season, the queue stretches out the door for freshly shucked Bluff oysters, battered oysters, and excellent fish and chips. It's utterly unpretentious — no tablecloths, no wine list, just the freshest Foveaux Strait seafood served fast. The most authentic Bluff oyster experience you can have.

reviews.summary: An Invercargill institution. The freshness of the oysters is unmatched. The queue can be long during peak season, but it moves steadily. No frills whatsoever.

pois.name: Bluff Oyster & Food Festival
pois.short: Annual May festival celebrating the iconic Bluff oyster — shucking competitions, live music and all-you-can-eat oysters.
pois.commentary: The Bluff Oyster & Food Festival is one of New Zealand's most beloved food events. Held each May in the tiny town of Bluff, it draws thousands for a day of oyster eating, shucking competitions, cooking demonstrations and live entertainment. Tickets sell out fast — often within minutes of going on sale. If you can't get a ticket, many Southland restaurants run oyster specials during festival week.

reviews.summary: A bucket-list event for oyster lovers. The atmosphere is festive and proudly local. Tickets sell out almost instantly — set a reminder.

pois.name: Fat Bastard Pies
pois.short: Invercargill's famous pie shop — the Big Nude Seafood chowder pie is a Southland icon.
pois.commentary: Fat Bastard Pies on Tay Street has built a cult following for its generously filled, creatively named pies. The Big Nude Seafood — stuffed with a homemade chowder of fresh fish, mussels and prawns — is the bestseller and a Southland icon. Other favourites include the Steak & Cheese and the Thai Chicken. It's takeaway-only, unpretentious and delicious. The perfect road-trip fuel for the drive to Milford Sound.

reviews.summary: The pies are enormous and delicious. The Big Nude Seafood lives up to the hype. Basic setting — this is grab-and-go comfort food at its best.

pois.name: The Pāua Shack
pois.short: Summer pop-up at Ocean Beach — street food made with pāua, oysters, crayfish and local fish.
pois.commentary: The Pāua Shack appears at Ocean Beach in Riverton during summer, serving Southland street food that showcases the region's incredible seafood. Pāua fritters, oyster po'boys, crayfish rolls and fresh fish tacos — all made with local catch. It's seasonal and casual, operating from a simple shack with picnic-table seating. The kind of food experience you can only have in the deep south of New Zealand.

reviews.summary: A unique summer experience. The pāua fritters are outstanding. Very weather-dependent and seasonal — check if it's open before making the trip.

pois.name: Batch Café
pois.short: Invercargill's best café — specialty coffee, seasonal brunch and the heart of the city's food scene.
pois.commentary: Batch has become the anchor of Invercargill's evolving food scene, serving specialty coffee and creative brunch dishes using local produce. The seasonal menu changes regularly — expect the kind of food you'd find in Wellington or Auckland, executed with care in NZ's southernmost city. The space is modern and welcoming, and the staff are genuinely passionate about food and coffee.

reviews.summary: The best coffee in Invercargill. The food quality surprises visitors who don't expect much from the deep south. A bright spot in the city centre.

pois.name: The Beach House Riverton
pois.short: Heritage beachfront restaurant in one of NZ's oldest settlements — fresh Foveaux Strait seafood.
pois.commentary: Riverton (one of the oldest European settlements in New Zealand) has this gem of a restaurant right on the beach. The menu celebrates Foveaux Strait seafood — blue cod, crayfish, pāua and Bluff oysters in season — in a heritage building with views across the bay. It's worth the 40-minute drive from Invercargill for the combination of history, setting and fresh southern seafood.

reviews.summary: The beachfront setting and fresh seafood are excellent. A worthy detour from the main tourist trail. Check winter hours before visiting.

pois.name: Invercargill Brewery
pois.short: NZ's southernmost brewery — Southland-brewed beers in a taproom that embraces the deep south.
pois.commentary: Invercargill Brewery has been crafting beers in NZ's southernmost city since 1999. The taproom serves the full range — the Pitch Black Stout and Stanley Green Pilsner are both excellent — alongside seasonal specials. The brewery embraces its southern identity, and the taproom has become a gathering spot for locals and travellers alike. A good counterpoint to the oyster-focused food scene.

reviews.summary: Solid beers with genuine local character. The taproom atmosphere is friendly. The Pitch Black Stout is a favourite among dark-beer fans.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara — Bird Sanctuary
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary — saddleback, yellowhead, Stewart Island robin, and weka in ancient forest.
pois.commentary: Ulva Island in Paterson Inlet, Stewart Island, is a predator-free open sanctuary where endangered birds thrive in ancient podocarp forest. Saddleback, yellowhead (mohua), Stewart Island robin, rifleman, and weka are easily seen on the walking tracks. Water taxi from Golden Bay wharf.

reviews.summary: NZ's finest bird island. The birds are incredibly tame. Allow at least 3 hours. Water taxi runs regularly.

pois.name: Stewart Island — Wild Kiwi Encounter
pois.short: See wild kiwi on the beach — Stewart Island tokoeka forage on the sand at night.
pois.commentary: Stewart Island / Rakiura is the only place in NZ where you can reliably see kiwi in the wild without a special setup. Stewart Island tokoeka come out to forage on Ocean Beach at night. Guided tours from Oban take you by water taxi and then walking to viewing spots.

reviews.summary: Life-changing kiwi experience. Seeing them on the beach is magical. Book the guided tour — it's worth every cent.

pois.name: Curio Bay — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Colony
pois.short: Yellow-eyed and little blue penguins alongside a 180-million-year-old petrified forest.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay in the Catlins hosts both yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) and little blue penguins, plus the remarkable Jurassic-era petrified forest on the rock platform. A DOC viewing hide allows close penguin observation without disturbance. Late afternoon is best for hoiho.

reviews.summary: Incredible combination of penguins and petrified forest. The DOC hide is well positioned. Free and uncrowded.

pois.name: Nugget Point — Seal & Seabird Colony
pois.short: Iconic lighthouse headland with fur seals, sea lions, and diverse seabird colonies on the 'nugget' rocks.
pois.commentary: Nugget Point in the Catlins is one of the South Island's most dramatic wildlife viewing locations. The rocky stacks ('nuggets') host NZ fur seals, elephant seals, royal spoonbill, and breeding spotted shag. The lighthouse walk offers panoramic views over the colony.

reviews.summary: Iconic NZ wildlife site. The lighthouse walk is a must. Binoculars essential for the seabird colonies.

pois.name: Catlins Coast — Hector's Dolphins
pois.short: Hector's dolphins play in the surf along the Catlins coast — watch from the beach at Porpoise Bay.
pois.commentary: Porpoise Bay in the Catlins is one of the most reliable places to see Hector's dolphins from shore. The small dolphins often surf the waves close to the beach, sometimes within metres of swimmers. The bay is also popular for surfing and has a beautiful sandy beach.

reviews.summary: Amazing to see dolphins from the beach. They come incredibly close. Best in summer. Free.

pois.name: Jackson Bay — Fiordland Crested Penguin
pois.short: NZ's rarest penguin nests in the rainforest-clad Fiordland coast — breeding season viewing July-November.
pois.commentary: The Fiordland crested penguin (tawaki) is NZ's rarest penguin, nesting in the dense rainforest along the Fiordland and West Coast coastlines. DOC monitoring sites offer limited viewing opportunities during breeding season. The penguins' forest nesting habitat makes them unique among penguin species.

reviews.summary: Rare and special penguin. Limited viewing opportunities. Breeding season only. Check with DOC for access.

pois.name: Lake Hauroko — Deep Lake Forest Birds
pois.short: NZ's deepest lake surrounded by pristine beech and podocarp forest — yellowhead, kaka, and robin.
pois.commentary: Lake Hauroko, at 462 metres NZ's deepest lake, is surrounded by untouched beech and podocarp forest. The birdlife is outstanding — yellowhead (mohua), kaka, South Island robin, rifleman, and brown creeper. Access is via a winding forestry road. The atmosphere of remoteness is powerful.

reviews.summary: Remote and beautiful. The birdlife is exceptional. The road in is rough but worth it. Few other visitors.

pois.name: Riverton Estuary — Wetland Birds
pois.short: Productive Southland estuary with bar-tailed godwit, royal spoonbill, and South Island pied oystercatcher.
pois.commentary: The Riverton (Aparima) Estuary is an important Southland wetland supporting diverse wading birds. Bar-tailed godwit arrive each spring from Alaska, joining resident royal spoonbill, pied oystercatcher, and pied stilt. Easy viewing from the estuary margins and the Riverton walkway.

reviews.summary: Good birding in a pleasant setting. The town of Riverton is worth exploring too. Low tide best for waders.

pois.name: Bluff Hill / Motupōhue — Seabird Viewing
pois.short: NZ's southernmost mainland seabird viewing — titi (sooty shearwater) streaming past at dusk in season.
pois.commentary: Bluff Hill lookout offers excellent seabird viewing from NZ's southernmost mainland point. During autumn, millions of titi (sooty shearwater) stream past heading to their breeding islands. NZ fur seals sometimes haul out below. The views to Stewart Island are panoramic.

reviews.summary: Great for seabird watching in season. The views to Stewart Island are worth the drive alone.

pois.name: Mason Bay — Wild Kiwi on the Beach
pois.short: Remote Stewart Island beach where kiwi forage in daylight — one of the world's great wildlife experiences.
pois.commentary: Mason Bay on Stewart Island's west coast is one of the few places where kiwi forage on the beach during daylight hours. The remote location requires a multi-hour tramp or charter flight. The beach is vast and wild, and the experience of watching kiwi foraging in the open is unparalleled.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's ultimate wildlife experiences. Daylight kiwi on the beach. Remote but absolutely worth the effort.

pois.name: Waiau River — Blue Duck Habitat
pois.short: Clean Fiordland river supporting endangered whio (blue duck) — NZ's rarest waterfowl.
pois.commentary: The Waiau River in Fiordland is one of the key South Island habitats for whio (blue duck). These rare ducks require clean, fast-flowing rivers with stable riverbanks for nesting. DOC's whio recovery programme operates trapping lines along the river to protect breeding pairs.

reviews.summary: Wild river setting. Whio sightings require patience and keen eyes. The river is beautiful regardless.

pois.name: Lake Wilkie — Bog Ecology Walk
pois.short: Boardwalk loop through a unique sphagnum bog ecosystem — podocarp succession in miniature.
pois.commentary: Lake Wilkie in the Catlins is a small glacial lake slowly being overtaken by sphagnum bog and podocarp forest. The short boardwalk loop demonstrates ecological succession beautifully — open water to sphagnum to shrubs to forest. Fernbird and tomtit in the surrounding bush.

reviews.summary: Fascinating ecological walk. Short and easy. Great for kids to learn about succession. Well-interpreted.

pois.name: Roaring Bay — Yellow-Eyed Penguin Hide
pois.short: DOC viewing hide overlooking a yellow-eyed penguin beach in the Catlins — late afternoon viewing.
pois.commentary: Roaring Bay near Nugget Point has a DOC viewing hide positioned to watch yellow-eyed penguins come ashore in late afternoon. The hide allows extended observation without disturbing the birds. NZ fur seals sometimes rest on the nearby rocks.

reviews.summary: Excellent free penguin viewing. The hide is well positioned. Patience rewarded in late afternoon.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound — Fiordland Wildlife
pois.short: Bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, and Fiordland crested penguins in NZ's most remote accessible fiord.
pois.commentary: Doubtful Sound is quieter and more remote than Milford, with excellent wildlife. A resident pod of about 60 bottlenose dolphins inhabits the fiord, NZ fur seals bask on the rocks, and Fiordland crested penguins nest along the shore. Overnight cruises offer the best wildlife encounters.

reviews.summary: More remote and intimate than Milford. The dolphins are almost always seen. Overnight cruise recommended.

pois.name: Omaui Penguin Haven
pois.short: Community-run penguin conservation project protecting little blue and yellow-eyed penguins near Invercargill.
pois.commentary: Omaui near Invercargill is a community-run penguin conservation project protecting both little blue and yellow-eyed penguins. Volunteer-maintained predator traps and nest boxes have boosted numbers. The community welcomes respectful visitors during penguin time (dusk for blues, late afternoon for hoiho).

reviews.summary: Wonderful community conservation. Small-scale and authentic. Contact the group for visiting advice.

pois.name: Te Anau Glowworm Caves
pois.short: Boat trip across Lake Te Anau to limestone caves filled with thousands of glowworms.
pois.commentary: The Te Anau Glowworm Caves are accessed by boat across Lake Te Anau. Inside, the limestone caves host thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa glowworms creating a stunning bioluminescent display. The cave system includes waterfalls, whirlpools, and a silent glowworm grotto.

reviews.summary: Magical experience. The boat trip adds to the atmosphere. Smaller and more intimate than Waitomo.

pois.name: New River Estuary / Oreti — Wading Birds
pois.short: Major Southland estuary with internationally significant wader populations — bar-tailed godwit and red knot.
pois.commentary: The New River Estuary near Invercargill is internationally significant for migratory wading birds. Thousands of bar-tailed godwit and red knot arrive from the Arctic each spring. Banded dotterel, wrybill, and South Island pied oystercatcher are also present. The Oreti Beach road provides access.

reviews.summary: Outstanding wader birding. Massive godwit flocks in spring/autumn. Scope essential. Best at low tide.

pois.name: Southland Museum and Art Gallery (Pyramid)
pois.short: Invercargill's iconic pyramid-shaped museum, famous for its tuatara breeding programme (check current status).
pois.commentary: The Southland Museum and Art Gallery is instantly recognisable for its striking pyramid-shaped building in Queens Park, Invercargill. The museum became nationally famous for its tuatara breeding programme — tuatara being ancient reptiles often called 'living fossils' that are endemic to New Zealand and can live well over 100 years. The museum housed Henry, a tuatara believed to be over 120 years old, who became a minor celebrity. The museum's collections cover Southland history, sub-Antarctic islands natural heritage, and regional art. Note: the museum building was closed in 2018 due to earthquake risk, and plans for its future have been under discussion. Check the current status before visiting, as some collections may have relocated to temporary venues. Even when closed, the building's unique pyramid architecture is worth seeing, and the surrounding Queens Park — Invercargill's 80-hectare public garden — features a rose garden, aviary, Japanese garden, and walking tracks.

reviews.summary: The pyramid building is iconic and the tuatara programme was wonderful. Check whether it's currently open before visiting as it's been closed for earthquake strengthening. Queens Park around it is lovely regardless.

pois.name: Waipapa Point Lighthouse
pois.short: Historic lighthouse marking NZ's worst civil maritime disaster site, now home to sea lion colonies.
pois.commentary: Waipapa Point Lighthouse stands at the southeastern tip of the South Island's Catlins coast, built in response to New Zealand's worst civil maritime disaster — the wreck of the SS Tararua in 1881, which claimed 131 lives on the treacherous reef offshore. The white wooden lighthouse, completed in 1884, was the last wooden lighthouse built in New Zealand and is now an automated navigation aid. The setting is dramatically wild and windswept, with crashing waves, rocky reefs, and sweeping views along the Catlins coastline. The area is home to a breeding colony of New Zealand sea lions — these magnificent marine mammals can often be seen hauled out on the beach or in the dunes near the lighthouse. Keep a safe distance (at least 10 metres) as sea lions can be aggressive. Interpretive panels tell the story of the Tararua disaster. The lighthouse is accessed via a short walk from the car park. Combine with the wider Catlins coastal drive for a full day exploring this remote and beautiful coastline.

reviews.summary: A moving and atmospheric heritage site with a tragic history. The sea lions are an unexpected bonus — we saw several on the beach. The Catlins setting is wild and beautiful. Worth the detour.

pois.name: Clifden Suspension Bridge
pois.short: Heritage suspension bridge from 1899 spanning the Waiau River, the longest of its type in NZ.
pois.commentary: The Clifden Suspension Bridge is one of Southland's most impressive heritage structures — a 111.5-metre suspended footbridge spanning the deep limestone gorge of the Waiau River near the settlement of Clifden. Built in 1899, it is the longest surviving single-span suspension bridge in New Zealand. The bridge was constructed to provide access for settlers on the remote western side of the Waiau River and features massive concrete anchor blocks and original ironwork. Walking across the narrow, swaying bridge is an exhilarating experience, with views down into the deep turquoise waters of the gorge below. The bridge is classified as a Category I historic place. Nearby, the Clifden Caves offer a self-guided exploration through a limestone cave system with glowworms (bring two torches per person). The bridge is located on the scenic route between Tuatapere and Lake Manapouri, making it a natural stop on the way to Fiordland. Free access at all times.

reviews.summary: A thrilling walk across a piece of history — the bridge sways noticeably and the gorge below is impressive. The nearby caves are a great bonus activity. A worthwhile stop on the Fiordland road.

pois.name: Oreti Beach
pois.short: Vast wild beach where Burt Munro tested his famous Indian motorcycle, stretching 26km along the Southland coast.
pois.commentary: Oreti Beach is a vast, dramatic sweep of sand stretching 26 kilometres along the Southland coast west of Invercargill. The beach is famous as the testing ground of Burt Munro, the legendary Invercargill speed racer whose story was told in 'The World's Fastest Indian' starring Anthony Hopkins. Munro used the beach's hard-packed sand to test his modified 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle before setting world speed records at Bonneville. The beach is driveable in many sections (4WD recommended), and locals still use it for land yachting, horse riding, and surfcasting. The wild Southern Ocean setting is spectacular — crashing waves, distant views to Stewart Island/Rakiura, and a dramatic sense of space and isolation. On clear days, the sunsets are extraordinary. The beach is exposed and can be very windy, but that is part of its wild character. Access is from Dunns Road, about 10 minutes west of Invercargill. Swimming is not recommended due to strong currents and cold temperatures. A quintessential Southland experience.

reviews.summary: An awe-inspiring wild beach — the scale is incredible and the Burt Munro connection adds romance. Not a swimming beach but the driving, walking, and sunset-watching are brilliant. So much space and drama.

pois.name: Wairaurahiri Jet
pois.short: Wilderness jet boat adventure through remote Fiordland river valleys, deep into untouched native bush.
pois.commentary: Wairaurahiri Jet offers one of New Zealand's most remote and spectacular jet boat experiences, operating on the Wairaurahiri River in the wilderness heartland of western Southland, on the edge of Fiordland National Park. The jet boat journey takes passengers deep into pristine native bush along a narrow, winding river that would be inaccessible by any other means. The dense podocarp forest, crystal-clear water, and absolute absence of development create a genuine wilderness experience. The operation also provides access to the South Coast Track and the remote settlement of Port Craig, linking to historic tramping routes. The Wairaurahiri is considerably more remote and intimate than the commercial jet boat operations in Queenstown — this is about wilderness immersion rather than pure speed thrills, though there is no shortage of exciting river navigation. Based near Tuatapere, bookings are essential as trips depend on river conditions and group numbers. Allow a full half-day. This is Southland adventure at its most authentic.

reviews.summary: An incredible wilderness jet boat experience — nothing like the commercial operations elsewhere. The remoteness and pristine bush are awe-inspiring. The driver's river knowledge is extraordinary. A true Fiordland adventure.

pois.name: Gore Big Brown Trout Sculpture
pois.short: Iconic giant brown trout sculpture in Gore, celebrating the town's claim as NZ's brown trout capital.
pois.commentary: The giant brown trout sculpture in Gore is one of New Zealand's classic 'Big Things' roadside attractions, celebrating the town's proud title as the brown trout capital of the world. Standing several metres tall on a central traffic island, the impressive fibreglass fish is a compulsory photo stop for travellers passing through this Southland farming town. Gore sits on the Mataura River, which is internationally recognised as one of the finest brown trout fly-fishing rivers in the world, attracting anglers from across the globe. The town takes its trout heritage seriously, with the Eastern Southland Gallery also housing a significant fly-fishing collection. Gore is also known as New Zealand's country music capital, hosting the annual Gold Guitar Awards. The combination of giant trout and country music gives this small Southland town an outsized personality. The sculpture is visible from the main highway through town — pull over safely for your photo. Nearby cafés provide refreshment for the ongoing road trip.

reviews.summary: A classic Kiwi roadside photo stop — the big trout never fails to make you smile. Not a destination in itself but a fun break on a Southland drive. Gore has more to offer than you'd expect.

pois.name: Curio Bay Fossil Forest & Penguins
pois.short: 180-million-year-old petrified forest on the Catlins coast, plus yellow-eyed penguins at dusk.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay on the southern Catlins coast is a site of extraordinary natural significance, combining a 180-million-year-old Jurassic fossil forest with a breeding colony of hoiho (yellow-eyed penguins). At low tide, the petrified stumps and fallen logs of an ancient forest are revealed on the wave-cut platform, dating to a time when New Zealand was part of the Gondwana supercontinent. Information panels explain the geology and identify tree species. In the adjacent Porpoise Bay, yellow-eyed penguins come ashore at dusk to reach their nesting sites, and a viewing hide allows observation without disturbance. Hector's dolphins are also frequently seen in the bay. The site is free to visit and managed by DOC. The Catlins is one of New Zealand's least-visited coastal regions, with a wild, untamed character. Curio Bay has basic camping facilities and a surf-lifesaving patrol in summer.

reviews.summary: A remarkable natural site combining ancient geology with precious wildlife. Seeing penguins waddle ashore at dusk is magical. The remoteness of the Catlins adds to the sense of discovery.

pois.name: Milford Road (SH94)
pois.short: One of the world's great scenic drives through Fiordland, from Te Anau to Milford Sound via the Homer Tunnel.
pois.commentary: The Milford Road (SH94) from Te Anau to Milford Sound is consistently rated among the world's most beautiful drives. The 119km journey takes you through a progression of dramatic landscapes: from pastoral farmland through ancient beech forest along the Eglinton Valley, past the mirror-calm surfaces of the Mirror Lakes, through the avalanche-prone Upper Hollyford valley, and finally through the remarkable Homer Tunnel, bored through solid granite at 945 metres elevation. Emerging from the tunnel, the road descends steeply into the Cleddau Valley with views of towering peaks and waterfalls. Essential stops include Mirror Lakes, The Chasm (a short walk to spectacular waterfalls), and the Pop's Viewpoint overlooking Lake Te Anau. Allow at least two hours each way without stops, more with walks. The road can close in winter due to avalanche risk, and chains may be required. Check conditions with Milford Road Alliance before departing.

reviews.summary: Simply one of the most spectacular drives on Earth. Every few kilometres brings a new jaw-dropping vista. Allow far more time than you think, as you will want to stop constantly for photos.

pois.name: Bluff Oyster & Food Trail
pois.short: Seafood trail in NZ's southernmost town, famous for Bluff oysters, paua, and the annual Bluff Oyster Festival.
pois.commentary: Bluff, at the southern tip of the South Island, is synonymous with the Bluff oyster (Tiostrea chilensis), widely regarded as the finest oyster in the world and harvested from the wild beds of Foveaux Strait. The oyster season runs from March to August, during which time fresh Bluff oysters are available at local venues and the famous Bluff Oyster & Food Festival, held annually in May, draws thousands of visitors. Beyond oysters, Bluff is a seafood destination year-round, with paua (abalone), blue cod, and crayfish featuring at local establishments. The town's Oyster World museum at the port tells the story of the oyster industry and Bluff's maritime heritage. Stirling Point, marked by the famous signpost showing distances to world capitals, is a must-photograph. The drive from Invercargill takes about 25 minutes along SH1. Even outside oyster season, Bluff's position at the end of the highway and its raw, windswept character make it a compelling destination.

reviews.summary: A pilgrimage for oyster lovers and a fascinating town at the end of the road. Fresh Bluff oysters in season are incomparable. The Oyster Festival is a memorable event worth planning around.

pois.name: Cathedral Caves
pois.short: Massive sea caves on the Catlins coast accessible only at low tide, with cathedral-like proportions.
pois.commentary: Cathedral Caves on the southern Catlins coast are among the largest sea caves in New Zealand, with the main cave reaching 30 metres high and penetrating 200 metres into the coastal cliff. The caves are accessible only at low tide, reached by a 30-minute walk through lush native bush from the car park. Inside, the scale is cathedral-like, with soaring ceilings, natural light filtering through openings, and the sound of waves echoing through the chambers. Multiple interconnected caves can be explored when conditions allow. The caves were formed by wave erosion of softer rock over millions of years. Access times are strictly controlled based on tide tables, and the entrance is gated during high tide for safety. Check opening times at the information centre or online before visiting. A small entry fee is charged. Located off the Southern Scenic Route between Papatowai and Curio Bay, the caves are a highlight of any Catlins coastal journey.

reviews.summary: An awe-inspiring natural wonder with genuinely cathedral-like proportions. The bush walk to the caves is lovely. Absolutely essential to check tide times before visiting, as access is limited.

pois.name: Stewart Island Ferry & Oban Village
pois.short: Ferry crossing to New Zealand's third island with wild kiwi, pristine bush, and the remote Oban village.
pois.commentary: The one-hour ferry crossing from Bluff to Stewart Island (Rakiura) delivers you to one of New Zealand's last great wilderness frontiers. Oban, the island's only settlement with around 400 permanent residents, is the gateway to Rakiura National Park, which covers 85 percent of the island. Stewart Island is one of the few places in New Zealand where kiwi can be seen in the wild, foraging on beaches at dusk. The Rakiura Track, one of New Zealand's Great Walks, is a three-day circuit through coastal bush and beaches. Short walks from Oban include the Ackers Point lighthouse track and the Observation Rock viewpoint. The island's remoteness means dark skies free from light pollution, and the aurora australis (Southern Lights) is occasionally visible. The ferry, operated by Real Journeys, runs several times daily from Bluff and can be rough in Foveaux Strait swells. Day trips are possible but an overnight stay is recommended. The island's pristine environment and relaxed pace feel like stepping back in time.

reviews.summary: An incredible wilderness experience on New Zealand's third island. Seeing wild kiwi on the beach at dusk is unforgettable. The ferry crossing can be rough, but the island rewards with pristine natural beauty.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound Wilderness Cruise
pois.short: Remote wilderness fiord cruise through Doubtful Sound with bottlenose dolphins, penguins, and profound silence.
pois.commentary: Doubtful Sound (Patea) is ten times longer and three times wider than Milford Sound, yet receives a fraction of the visitors, creating a truly wilderness fiord experience. Access requires a boat across Lake Manapouri and a bus over Wilmot Pass, adding to the sense of remoteness. The fiord is home to a resident pod of around 60 bottlenose dolphins, frequently encountered during cruises. Fiordland crested penguins nest in the outer reaches, and fur seals bask on rocky islets. The silence is a defining feature, with guides cutting engines to allow passengers to experience the profound quiet of this remote wilderness. The fiord's three arms create a complex waterway surrounded by steep, bush-clad mountains rising to over 1,000 metres. Overnight cruises aboard small vessels offer the most immersive experience, with kayaking and tender boat exploration of side arms. Day cruises from Manapouri take around 8 hours including transfers. The remoteness and quiet grandeur make Doubtful Sound a more powerful experience than its famous neighbour for many visitors.

reviews.summary: A more intimate and wild fiord experience than Milford Sound. The dolphins are a highlight and the silence is extraordinary. The journey to get there adds to the adventure and sense of remoteness.

pois.name: Curio Bay Fossil Forest
pois.short: 180-million-year-old petrified forest on the Catlins coast with penguin viewing.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay on the Catlins coast is home to one of the world's most extensive and accessible petrified forests, dating back approximately 180 million years to the Jurassic period. At low tide, the fossilised remains of trees, ferns, and other plants are clearly visible in the rock platform, offering a remarkable window into New Zealand's ancient past when the land was part of the Gondwana supercontinent. The adjacent Porpoise Bay is home to a resident colony of Hector's dolphins and yellow-eyed penguins that come ashore at dusk. A DOC viewing platform allows penguin watching without disturbance. Information panels explain the geological significance. The site is free to access. The Catlins coast is remote, so allow plenty of driving time and fill your fuel tank in Invercargill or Owaka.

reviews.summary: A remarkable geological site combined with wildlife viewing. The fossilised trees are fascinating. The penguins at dusk are a highlight. Remote but worth the journey.

pois.name: Bill Richardson Transport World
pois.short: The world's largest collection of restored classic trucks and transport memorabilia, housed in a purpose-built Invercargill museum.
pois.commentary: Bill Richardson Transport World is a labour of love that has grown into one of New Zealand's most impressive private museums. The collection spans over 300 restored vehicles, from vintage trucks and fire engines to quirky petrol pumps and automobile art. Interactive exhibits let visitors climb into cabs, sound horns, and explore the evolution of road transport. The museum sits in Invercargill's industrial quarter and draws car enthusiasts and families alike. Allow at least two hours — there is far more here than first meets the eye.

reviews.summary: A world-class transport museum that surprises everyone who walks through the door. The sheer scale and quality of restoration is extraordinary. Even non-petrolheads leave impressed by the passion on display.

pois.name: Classic Motorcycle Mecca
pois.short: The Southern Hemisphere's leading motorcycle museum — over 300 beautifully curated machines spanning a century of two-wheeled history.
pois.commentary: Classic Motorcycle Mecca houses more than 300 motorcycles in a beautifully lit, gallery-style space that treats each machine as a work of art. The collection ranges from pioneering early-1900s bikes to iconic marques like Brough Superior, Indian, and Vincent. Themed displays explore the cultural impact of motorcycling, from wartime dispatch riders to cafe racers and the open-road dream. Located in central Invercargill, it sits just minutes from Bill Richardson Transport World, making for a superb double-header for anyone with petrol in their veins. The gift shop stocks quality motorcycle memorabilia.

reviews.summary: A stunning museum that elevates motorcycles to art. The curation and lighting are world-class. Pairs perfectly with Transport World nearby for a full day of Invercargill motor culture.

pois.name: Rakiura Museum, Stewart Island
pois.short: Stewart Island's cultural heart — Maori history, whaling heritage, and European settlement told through a modern, well-curated facility.
pois.commentary: Rakiura Museum sits in the heart of Oban, Stewart Island's only settlement, and tells the layered story of New Zealand's third island. Displays cover early Maori occupation and the importance of titi (muttonbird) harvesting, the rough-and-ready whaling and sealing era, and the European sawmillers and fishermen who followed. A highlight is the collection of historical photographs showing just how remote and self-reliant this community has always been. The museum is small but rich in detail, and the volunteer staff are often descendants of the families whose stories fill the cases. Well worth a visit before heading out on the Rakiura Track or the boat to Ulva Island.

reviews.summary: A small but richly detailed museum that brings Stewart Island's remarkable history to life. The volunteer guides add personal stories you won't find in any book. Essential context before exploring the island.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara
pois.short: An open predator-free bird sanctuary in Paterson Inlet — walk among fearless kaka, saddleback, and weka on ancient forest trails.
pois.commentary: Ulva Island is one of New Zealand's most accessible predator-free sanctuaries and one of the few places where you can walk freely among native birds that show almost no fear of humans. A short water taxi from Golden Bay wharf on Stewart Island delivers you to a 267-hectare island of ancient podocarp forest that has been predator-free since 1997. Kaka land on branches beside you, Stewart Island robin hop at your feet, and yellowhead (mohua) and saddleback call from the canopy. The bush itself is magnificent — rimu, rata, and kamahi draped in mosses and ferns. Well-formed tracks loop through the island in two to four hours. No facilities exist on the island, so bring water and food, and carry everything out.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's most magical wildlife experiences. The fearless birds are unlike anything on the mainland. The ancient forest is cathedral-like in its beauty and silence.

pois.name: Key Summit Track
pois.short: A stunning side trip off the Routeburn Track — alpine wetlands, tarns, and 360-degree views of three major valleys.
pois.commentary: Key Summit is one of Fiordland's finest day walks and one of the few places where you can reach the alpine zone without a full multi-day tramp. The track branches off the Routeburn near The Divide shelter on the Milford Road and climbs steadily through beech forest to an alpine wetland boardwalk at 919 metres. From the summit, views extend across the Hollyford, Eglinton, and Greenstone valleys — a panorama that captures Fiordland's immense scale. Alpine tarns reflect the surrounding peaks on calm days. The return walk takes about three hours. The car park at The Divide fills early in summer, so start before 9 am. This is one of the best short walks in the entire national park.

reviews.summary: An accessible taste of the alpine zone with rewards far beyond the effort required. The three-valley panorama is breathtaking. Start early to secure parking at The Divide.

pois.name: Te Anau Glowworm Caves
pois.short: An underground cave system on Lake Te Anau accessed by boat — thousands of glowworms glitter in the darkness like a subterranean night sky.
pois.commentary: The Te Anau Glowworm Caves sit on the western shore of Lake Te Anau, reached by a scenic 30-minute boat ride across the lake. Inside, a series of limestone passages carved by water over millennia lead to a glowworm grotto where thousands of Arachnocampa luminosa larvae light up the cave ceiling in an ethereal blue-green display. Small boats glide silently through the grotto, allowing visitors to float beneath the living constellations. The caves were only rediscovered in 1948 by local explorer Lawson Burrows. Tours depart from the Real Journeys visitor centre in Te Anau and run multiple times daily. The experience lasts about two and a half hours including the lake crossing. Bookings are essential in summer.

reviews.summary: A magical subterranean experience that feels like floating beneath a starlit sky. The boat ride across Lake Te Anau adds to the atmosphere. Book ahead in peak season as tours sell out.

pois.name: Te Anau Bird Sanctuary / Wildlife Centre
pois.short: A DOC-run lakeside sanctuary where you can see the takahe — the flightless bird thought extinct until its dramatic rediscovery in 1948.
pois.commentary: The Te Anau Bird Sanctuary sits on the shore of Lake Te Anau, a short walk from the town centre, and is run by the Department of Conservation. Its star residents are takahe — large, brilliantly coloured flightless birds that were believed extinct for 50 years before being rediscovered in the Murchison Mountains behind Te Anau in 1948. The sanctuary also houses other rare native species including whio (blue duck), kaka, and morepork. Aviaries are set in native bush beside the lake, creating a peaceful environment. Entry is free, making it an easy and rewarding stop for anyone passing through Te Anau. Interpretive panels explain the conservation programmes that have slowly pulled the takahe back from the brink.

reviews.summary: A free and deeply rewarding wildlife experience right in town. Seeing a living takahe is a privilege — their story of rediscovery is one of conservation's great tales. The lakeside setting is lovely.

pois.name: Queens Park, Invercargill
pois.short: An 80-hectare green oasis in the heart of Invercargill with rose gardens, a Japanese garden, walk-through aviary, and glasshouse winter gardens.
pois.commentary: Queens Park is Invercargill's crown jewel — 80 hectares of manicured gardens, native bush, and recreational facilities in the centre of the city. Highlights include a formal rose garden, a serene Japanese garden, a walk-through aviary with native and exotic birds, and heated winter gardens filled with tropical plants and cacti. A small animal enclosure with deer and wallabies delights younger visitors. The park also contains a golf course, tennis courts, and a cafe. Established in 1857, it is one of New Zealand's oldest public parks and a testament to the civic pride of Southland. On a fine day, it is one of the most pleasant urban green spaces in the South Island.

reviews.summary: A surprisingly beautiful and expansive park for a city of Invercargill's size. The winter gardens and aviary are highlights. A perfect place to stretch your legs between long Southland drives.

pois.name: Tuatara Encounter at Southland Museum
pois.short: Meet tuatara — ancient reptiles that predate the dinosaurs and are found nowhere else on Earth.
pois.commentary: The tuatara is not a lizard but the sole surviving member of an order that thrived alongside the dinosaurs over 200 million years ago. Southland Museum's tuatara programme has been one of the longest-running captive breeding efforts in New Zealand, producing offspring that have been released to predator-free islands. Visitors can observe these remarkable creatures up close — their slow deliberate movements and ancient features make them utterly unlike any other living reptile. Henry, the oldest resident, is estimated to be well over 120 years old and became a father for the first time at 111. The display provides excellent interpretation of tuatara biology and the conservation challenges they face. Check current opening arrangements before visiting.

reviews.summary: A rare chance to see one of the planet's most ancient creatures. Henry the tuatara is a celebrity. Check opening hours as availability can vary.

pois.name: Gertrude Saddle Track
pois.short: A challenging day walk to a saddle high above the Milford Sound basin — spectacular rewards for experienced trampers only.
pois.commentary: The Gertrude Saddle route is one of Fiordland's most dramatic day walks, climbing from the Gertrude Valley car park on the Milford Road to a narrow saddle at around 1,400 metres with staggering views down into the Milford Sound basin. This is not a beginner's walk — the upper section involves steep, exposed rock scrambling, route-finding through boulder fields, and potentially snow and ice even in summer. The reward is a vista that few day-walkers in New Zealand can match: the full length of Milford Sound laid out below, flanked by sheer peaks. Weather changes rapidly at altitude, and the route should only be attempted in settled conditions by fit, experienced trampers with proper gear. DOC classifies this as a route, not a track — there are no markers above the bushline. Allow six to eight hours return.

reviews.summary: One of the most spectacular viewpoints in all of New Zealand — but you earn every metre. Only for experienced, well-equipped trampers in good weather. The view into Milford Sound from above is unforgettable.

pois.name: Lake Hauroko
pois.short: New Zealand's deepest lake at 462 metres — remote, wild, and surrounded by untouched Fiordland bush.
pois.commentary: Lake Hauroko is New Zealand's deepest lake, plunging 462 metres into the bedrock of western Southland. It sits at the southern edge of Fiordland National Park, reached by a 30-kilometre gravel road from Clifden. The lake is 30 kilometres long and occupies a glacially carved trough surrounded by dense, unbroken native forest. There are no commercial operations on the lake — just a basic boat ramp, a DOC campsite, and profound silence. On a still day the reflections of the surrounding bush in the dark water are extraordinary. The lake is also the starting point for the Hump Ridge Track and the South Coast Track. Sandflies can be ferocious. Bring insect repellent and be prepared for changeable weather even in summer.

reviews.summary: A genuinely wild and remote place that feels like the edge of the world. The depth and stillness of the water are mesmerising. Come prepared for sandflies and solitude.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima Heritage Trail
pois.short: One of New Zealand's oldest European settlements — a heritage trail through a charming coastal fishing village with stories stretching back to the 1830s.
pois.commentary: Riverton (Aparima) claims to be one of New Zealand's oldest permanent European settlements, with whalers and sealers establishing themselves here from the early 1830s. The heritage trail winds through the village, passing historic buildings, the old whaling station site, and the excellent Te Hikoi Southern Journey museum. The town sits at the mouth of the Aparima River where it meets Foveaux Strait, and retains a quiet, unhurried charm that bigger towns have lost. The local fish and chips are legendary — Riverton's fishing fleet still lands fresh blue cod daily. It makes a rewarding stop on the Southern Scenic Route between Invercargill and the Catlins, and the combination of history, coast, and good kai is hard to beat.

reviews.summary: A charming coastal village with genuine history and excellent fish and chips. The heritage trail is well-signed and the Te Hikoi museum is a hidden gem. A lovely break on the Southern Scenic Route.

pois.name: Bluff & Bluff Oyster Trail
pois.short: Home of the famous Bluff oyster — visit Stirling Point at the mainland's southernmost tip and feast on the ocean's finest.
pois.commentary: Bluff is the southernmost town on the New Zealand mainland and the spiritual home of the Bluff oyster (Tiostrea chilensis), widely regarded as one of the finest oysters in the world. The season runs from March to August, when the dredge boats work the cold waters of Foveaux Strait. Oyster lovers make pilgrimages here to eat them fresh — raw, battered, or in chowder — at local cafes and the annual Bluff Oyster & Food Festival in May. Beyond the oysters, Stirling Point marks the end of State Highway 1 and features the famous signpost showing distances to global cities. The view across Foveaux Strait to Stewart Island is worth the drive alone. The Bluff Hill / Motupohue lookout offers 360-degree views over the strait, the Tiwai aluminium smelter, and the Southland plains.

reviews.summary: A pilgrimage for oyster lovers and a satisfying end-of-the-road experience at Stirling Point. The oysters in season are sublime. The signpost photo is obligatory.

pois.name: Hollyford Track to Martins Bay
pois.short: An epic wilderness track from the mountains to the remote Martins Bay coast — one of Fiordland's great multi-day adventures.
pois.commentary: The Hollyford Track follows the Hollyford River from its valley near the Milford Road all the way to the wild Martins Bay coast on the Tasman Sea — one of the lowest-level routes through Fiordland and one of the most remote. The 56-kilometre track passes through dense rainforest, crosses swing bridges over milky-blue rivers, and emerges at a coast that has not had a permanent settlement since the 1870s. DOC huts are spaced along the route for independent trampers (3-4 days one way). Guided trips with Hollyford Wilderness Experience include jet boat sections and a flight out from Martins Bay, compressing the walk to three days. Fur seals and Fiordland crested penguins inhabit the coast. The track is low-altitude and can be walked year-round, though winter brings shorter days and higher river levels.

reviews.summary: A true wilderness journey from mountains to coast through some of the most untouched landscape in New Zealand. The guided option with jet boats and scenic flight is exceptional. Remote and raw — exactly what Fiordland does best.

pois.name: Curio Bay Petrified Forest & Penguins
pois.short: A 180-million-year-old petrified forest exposed on the beach — plus yellow-eyed penguins come ashore at dusk.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay on the Catlins coast combines two extraordinary natural features in one location. At low tide, the rock platform reveals the stumps and fallen trunks of a 180-million-year-old Jurassic forest, petrified in place when volcanic mud buried the trees and mineralised them over millennia. The fossilised remains are remarkably clear — you can see bark patterns, growth rings, and root systems in the stone. At dusk, the adjacent Porpoise Bay beach becomes a runway for yellow-eyed penguins (hoiho) returning from the sea to their nesting sites in the coastal scrub. A viewing hide allows observation without disturbance. The combination of deep-time geology and living wildlife makes this one of the most remarkable free-access sites in New Zealand. The Catlins are remote, so allow plenty of driving time.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary combination of ancient geology and living wildlife. The petrified forest at low tide is fascinating, and the penguins at dusk are a privilege to watch. Free access in a wild, remote setting.

pois.name: Nugget Point Lighthouse / Tokatā
pois.short: A lighthouse perched on a narrow headland above sea stacks teeming with seals, sea lions, penguins and seabirds.
pois.commentary: A narrow headland topped by an 1870 lighthouse, with rocky islets supporting fur seals, Hooker’s sea lions, penguins, and nesting seabirds.

reviews.summary: The combination of dramatic scenery and abundant wildlife is outstanding.

pois.name: Curio Bay Petrified Forest
pois.short: A 180-million-year-old petrified forest exposed on a wave-cut platform — plus yellow-eyed penguins nesting in the dunes.
pois.commentary: At low tide, fossilised remains of a Jurassic forest are revealed. Nearby, yellow-eyed penguins nest and Hector’s dolphins swim in the bay.

reviews.summary: The petrified forest is genuinely mind-blowing. The penguin viewing is a bonus.

pois.name: Purakaunui Falls
pois.short: A three-tiered waterfall in native bush — one of the most photographed waterfalls in New Zealand.
pois.commentary: A 20-metre tiered waterfall cascading over three distinct rock shelves. A 10-minute track leads to the viewing platform.

reviews.summary: Extraordinarily photogenic. The short walk through native bush adds to the experience.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound / Patea
pois.short: Three times longer and ten times the area of Milford — and a fraction of the visitors.
pois.commentary: Access is via a boat across Lake Manapōuri, then a bus over Wilmot Pass. Home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins, fur seals and Fiordland crested penguins.

reviews.summary: More powerful than Milford because of the silence and lack of crowds.

pois.name: Southland Museum & Art Gallery — Tuatarium, Invercargill
pois.short: The country's largest living tuatara collection, housed under the iconic white pyramid of Invercargill's museum.
pois.commentary: The Southland Museum and Art Gallery occupies the striking white pyramidal building in Invercargill's Queens Park — the only purpose-built tuatarium in the world, maintained as a breeding programme for tuatara (*Sphenodon punctatus*), the ancient reptile that pre-dates the dinosaurs. The museum also houses the Sub-Antarctic Heritage Gallery, the most comprehensive display in New Zealand covering the ecology, exploration history, and World Heritage significance of the five New Zealand subantarctic island groups. Note: the museum building was severely damaged by a 2018 roof failure and has been undergoing staged reopening — the tuatarium is operational but check specific gallery hours before visiting.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Southland Museum's tuatara breeding programme as one of the most extraordinary wildlife experiences in New Zealand — seeing the 'living fossil' at close range in an indoor setting is unlike any zoo encounter. The Sub-Antarctic gallery is consistently called the best summary of that remote world available anywhere. Reviewers note to check the current building status before visiting.

pois.name: Bill Richardson Transport World, Invercargill
pois.short: The world's largest private vehicle collection — 400+ vintage cars, trucks, and motorcycles displayed in a custom-built Invercargill complex.
pois.commentary: Bill Richardson Transport World holds a Guinness World Record as the world's largest single-owner vehicle collection — over 400 vehicles including motorcycles, fire engines, American cars, and working steam traction engines, all displayed in a purpose-built 23,000 m² complex on Dee Street, Invercargill. Bill Richardson (1936–2017) was a successful Southland trucking entrepreneur who spent six decades accumulating vehicles. The complex includes a diner, gift shop, and a large toy and collectables gallery. Entry charge, but widely regarded as worth it. The adjacent Classic Motorcycle Mecca (same complex) holds over 200 motorcycles across 125 years of production. Unexpectedly one of New Zealand's best transport museums.

reviews.summary: Visitors who arrive sceptical about a vehicle museum describe Transport World as genuinely jaw-dropping — the scale, curation, and variety of the collection exceeds all expectations. Classic car and motorcycle enthusiasts describe it as a bucket-list pilgrimage. Reviewers recommend allowing at least two hours and note the on-site diner is worthwhile.

pois.name: Invercargill Brewing Company
pois.short: One of New Zealand's original craft breweries — Invercargill Brewing has been producing idiosyncratic Southland beers since 1999.
pois.commentary: Invercargill Brewing Company was founded in 1999 by Steve Nally — one of the first wave of New Zealand microbreweries and consistently one of the country's most individual operations. The brewery is best known for its Pitchblack Stout (a near-pitch-black dry stout that became a national standard), Biman Bomber (a strong ale), and a range that rotates with seasonal and experimental brews. The taproom on Leet Street is compact and unpretentious. For beer travellers, Invercargill Brewing is a pilgrimage stop — its longevity and independence at the bottom of the South Island are genuinely admirable. Pairs with the Waxy O'Shea's Irish bar around the corner for a full Invercargill evening.

reviews.summary: Beer reviewers consistently cite Invercargill Brewing as one of New Zealand's essential craft-beer stops — the longevity and quality of the Pitchblack Stout is called a national treasure. The taproom is described as unpretentious and welcoming. Reviewers note the brewery's survival in Invercargill for 25+ years is itself an achievement worth toasting.

pois.name: Burt Munro Challenge — Oreti Beach Speed Week
pois.short: Oreti Beach — the 20 km hard-sand strand where Burt Munro tested his Indian Scout. Now the site of the annual Burt Munro Challenge speed event.
pois.commentary: Oreti Beach is a 20 km arc of hard compacted sand southwest of Invercargill, backed by marram-grass dunes. It is swimmable (the currents are dangerous — local knowledge only) but its cultural significance is as Burt Munro's training ground. Munro, the subject of the 2005 Anthony Hopkins film *The World's Fastest Indian*, used Oreti Beach to test and develop his 1920 Indian Scout motorcycle through the 1950s–60s before taking it to Bonneville. The beach is still driveable for most of its length. The annual Burt Munro Challenge (held every November in Invercargill/Oreti) is New Zealand's largest motorcycle event, drawing 2,000+ bikes. Outside event week, the beach is vast, empty, and atmospheric.

reviews.summary: Visitors with any knowledge of the Burt Munro story describe Oreti Beach as one of the most evocative sites in New Zealand — the sheer scale of the sand and the knowledge of the old man testing his modified motorcycle here generates a specific kind of feeling. Reviewers note the beach is best at low tide on a clear day. The annual Challenge week transforms the whole region.

pois.name: Fiordland Brewing Company, Te Anau
pois.short: Fiordland's own craft brewery in Te Anau — a taproom and restaurant that is the essential post-track decompression stop.
pois.commentary: Fiordland Brewing Company has been operating a taproom on Te Anau's lakefront since 2014 — the only craft brewery in the Fiordland gateway. The year-round taps include the Takahē Ale (named after the rare bird at the Te Anau bird park), Plinian IPA, and a rotating seasonal programme. The kitchen serves proper food — venison burgers, blue cod, Fiordland lamb. After completing the Milford Track, Kepler Track, or any other multi-day Great Walk, the combination of a first cold beer and a serious meal at Fiordland Brewing has become a significant local ritual. Small, unpretentious, and genuinely local.

reviews.summary: Track walkers describe Fiordland Brewing as the perfect end to a Fiordland Great Walk — cold beer, real food, and a taproom atmosphere that celebrates the landscape outside the window. The Takahē Ale is called well-named and well-made. Reviewers note the kitchen is better than expected for a craft-brewery pub.

pois.name: Vrakas / Seriously Good Chocolate Co., Invercargill
pois.short: Invercargill's award-winning artisan chocolate maker — bean-to-bar South American single-origin bars and confectionery, factory shop and café.
pois.commentary: Seriously Good Chocolate Company on Tay Street, Invercargill, has been making artisan chocolate since 2012 — one of a small number of genuine bean-to-bar producers in New Zealand, sourcing directly from Ecuador and Peru and processing on-site. The factory shop and café offers tasting, barista coffee, and a full range of single-origin bars and filled chocolates. The company has won multiple Academy of Chocolate and New Zealand Fine Food awards. Invercargill has a quiet but growing artisan food scene — Seriously Good Chocolate is its most export-minded expression. A 45-minute stop that pays disproportionate dividends.

reviews.summary: Reviewers describe Seriously Good Chocolate as a complete surprise in Invercargill — genuinely world-class bean-to-bar chocolate being made at the bottom of New Zealand. The factory shop tastings are described as educational and generous. Most reviewers leave with more chocolate than planned and describe this as exactly appropriate.

pois.name: Catlins Coast — Owaka & Catlins Museum
pois.short: Owaka — the Catlins' only town, with a small museum that holds the full story of this remote, forested, wildly beautiful coast.
pois.commentary: Owaka (population ~350) is the service hub for the Catlins — the wild forested coast between the Clutha River and the Southland border, administered across the Otago-Southland boundary. The Catlins Museum at Owaka covers the full arc of Catlins history: Kāi Tahu occupation, the shore-based whaling era, the 1860s–1920s podocarp timber industry (the Catlins was logged comprehensively for its rimu and kahikatea), and the subsequent recovery and conservation. The museum is small, volunteer-staffed, and genuinely informative. Owaka is also where the Southern Scenic Route traveller gets petrol and food before heading deeper into the Catlins — use the stop well.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Catlins Museum as the essential orientation stop before driving deeper into the coast — the context it provides for the logging history transforms what might seem like simply beautiful forest into a landscape of recovery and resilience. Reviewers praise the volunteer staff as knowledgeable and genuinely passionate. Best combined with lunch at the Owaka café.

pois.name: Hokonui Moonshine Museum, Gore
pois.short: The museum dedicated to the illicit whisky distilled in the Hokonui Hills — Southland's prohibition-era moonshine tradition, told with unashamed local pride.
pois.commentary: The Hokonui Hills above Gore were the epicentre of New Zealand's most celebrated illegal whisky industry. Scottish settlers, using knowledge brought from the Highlands, began illicit distilling here in the 1860s. The practice continued through the prohibition era (1900–1953 in this district) and into the 1950s. At peak, some 30 stills were operating in the hills; the Hokonui whisky was renowned as far as Dunedin, where it was served in the better hotels under polite euphemisms. The museum in Gore holds the equipment, the court records, and the photographs — and sells a legitimate commercial recreation of the original recipe. A genuinely Southland story, told without apology.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Hokonui Museum as one of the most entertaining small museums in New Zealand — the combination of genuine history, local pride, and the absurdity of a province that maintained illegal distilling for 90 years makes for a museum with real personality. The commercial Hokonui whisky sale at the exit is consistently called the most logical museum gift shop in the country.

pois.name: Gore Country Music Festival & Guitar Statue
pois.short: Gore — New Zealand's self-proclaimed 'Country Music Capital', with a giant guitar statue and an annual festival that fills the town with real devotion.
pois.commentary: Gore, population ~12,000, is the unlikely capital of New Zealand country music — a tradition rooted in the Scots-Irish pastoral farming culture of Southland that has been institutionalised since the 1970s. The International Gold Guitar Awards (held annually in late May/June) is the country's premier country music festival, drawing both domestic and international artists. A 2-m stainless steel guitar sculpture marks the town entrance. The Eastern Southland Gallery in Gore is, separately, one of New Zealand's strongest regional art galleries — the Ralph Hotere collection alone (twelve major works by New Zealand's most celebrated contemporary artist) justifies the stop for art visitors.

reviews.summary: Visitors who arrive in Gore expecting little describe it as a genuine surprise — the country music identity is authentic and fully inhabited, and the Eastern Southland Gallery is consistently rated one of New Zealand's best regional art institutions by reviewers with specialist art knowledge. The Hotere collection is described as a reason to come to Gore regardless of any music interest.

pois.name: Eastern Southland Gallery & Ralph Hotere Collection, Gore
pois.short: One of New Zealand's finest regional galleries — and home to the most significant Ralph Hotere collection open to the public.
pois.commentary: The Eastern Southland Gallery in Gore holds what is arguably the most important publicly accessible collection of works by Ralph Hotere (Te Aupōuri, 1931–2013) — consistently ranked among New Zealand's two or three greatest visual artists. Twelve major works from different periods of Hotere's career are on permanent display, including large black paintings from the 1970s–80s that reference the Aramoana massacre, anti-nuclear protest, and the destruction of his Portobello studio. The wider gallery collection includes John Walsh's world-class African art collection and a strong regional photography archive. Entry is free. Most New Zealand art visitors who find Gore's gallery consider it one of the best-kept secrets in the country.

reviews.summary: Art reviewers and general visitors alike describe the Eastern Southland Gallery as genuinely world-class for a small regional institution — the Hotere collection alone justifies a Southland detour. Free entry and well-presented permanent works are consistently praised. Reviewers note the African art collection is an entirely unexpected bonus.

pois.name: Waiau River & Clifden Suspension Bridge
pois.short: New Zealand's longest historic suspension bridge — an 1899 wire cable bridge over the Waiau River, still walkable, on the way to the Fiordland edge.
pois.commentary: The Clifden Suspension Bridge over the Waiau River near Tuatapere is New Zealand's longest remaining historic suspension bridge — 111 m of original 1899 wire cable and timber plank construction, now pedestrian-only. The Waiau is the wild river that drains Lake Te Anau and Lake Manapōuri and runs to the sea at the Waiau Estuary near Riverton. The bridge was a critical crossing for pastoral farming in western Southland; it served vehicle traffic until the 1960s. Today it connects the Clifden Caves (limestone caverns, self-guided) on the east bank. A short side trip from the Tuatapere-Te Anau road, and one of the most photogenic heritage structures in Southland.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe the Clifden Bridge as the best-kept heritage secret in western Southland — a genuinely impressive and well-preserved engineering structure in a beautiful river setting. The combination with the Clifden limestone caves on the same stop makes it a 2-hour pause well worth building into a Tuatapere itinerary. Reviewers note the bridge is walkable but bouncy.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima — Southland's Oldest European Settlement
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest surviving European settlement — Riverton was a shore whaling station from 1836, predating Dunedin by 12 years.
pois.commentary: Riverton — Aparima in Māori — on the shore of Foveaux Strait, 38 km west of Invercargill, has a credible claim to being New Zealand's oldest continuously inhabited European settlement. A shore whaling station was operating here from at least 1836 — predating Dunedin (1848) by over a decade. The settlement was mixed from the beginning: European and American whalers, and Kāi Tahu women who married into the industry. The Te Hikoi Southland Museum at Riverton covers this history. Riverton is a small, strongly-character-maintained coastal town with good cafés, the Aparima River estuary birdlife, and a beach that is popular with local families but largely unknown to tourists.

reviews.summary: Visitors who discover Riverton describe it as one of the most authentic and understated towns on the South Island's heritage circuit — the whaling history is well told at the Te Hikoi museum, and the town itself is genuinely inhabited rather than curated for tourists. Reviewers recommend it as a lunch stop between Invercargill and Tuatapere. The estuary birdlife is a bonus.

pois.name: Te Rūnanga o Awarua — Awarua / Bluff Māori Cultural Landscape
pois.short: Awarua — the Ngāi Tahu rūnanga of Bluff, whose tikanga governs the annual tītī (muttonbird) harvest on the Titi Islands of Foveaux Strait.
pois.commentary: Te Rūnanga o Awarua is the Kāi Tahu papatipu rūnanga based at Bluff, whose rohe (territory) covers Foveaux Strait and the Titi Islands to the east of Stewart Island. The annual tītī harvest — conducted each April–May under a customary permit system exclusive to Kāi Tahu rights holders — is one of the few surviving examples of a continuous, intergenerational Māori resource-harvest tradition in New Zealand. The harvest of sooty shearwater (tītī / muttonbird) chicks has been conducted for hundreds of years, using knowledge of specific island rookeries held within specific Kāi Tahu families. The marae complex at Awarua (near Bluff township) is not a tourist facility, but the historical and cultural dimension of Awarua is covered with care in the Te Hikoi Museum at Riverton and the interpretation panels at the Stirling Point signpost.

reviews.summary: Visitors who engage with the Awarua tītī tradition describe it as one of the most remarkable examples of continuous indigenous resource management in Oceania — the specific family rights to specific island burrows maintained across generations is called extraordinary by reviewers with anthropological knowledge. Respectful background reading before visiting the area is consistently recommended.

pois.name: Kepler Track Trailhead — Lake Te Anau Great Walk
pois.short: The Fiordland Great Walk that starts and ends at Te Anau — alpine ridge, limestone bluffs, and beech forest in a four-day circuit.
pois.commentary: The Kepler Track is a 60 km, four-day Great Walk circuit from Te Anau — the only Great Walk in Fiordland that begins and ends in the same town without requiring transport. The track climbs through beech forest to the exposed alpine ridge of the Kepler Mountains, with 360° views of the Fiordland ranges, then descends via the Iris Burn valley and follows the edge of Lakes Te Anau and Manapōuri back to town. Two huts on the alpine ridge (Luxmore and Iris Burn) are the most dramatically situated Great Walk accommodation in Fiordland. DOC booking required March–November.

reviews.summary: Walkers describe the Kepler Track as the most logistically straightforward of the Fiordland Great Walks — the same-town start and finish eliminates the shuttle complexity of Milford and Routeburn. The alpine ridge section above the bushline is called the finest day of walking in Fiordland by many reviewers. The limestone bluffs near the Luxmore summit are consistently called a surprise highlight.

pois.name: Slope Point — Southernmost Point of South Island
pois.short: The southernmost point of the South Island — a 20-minute farm walk to a windswept cliff above the Southern Ocean, with a signpost to the equator.
pois.commentary: Slope Point is the southernmost tip of the South Island — not quite New Zealand's southernmost land (Stewart Island/Rakiura lies further south), but the mainland's lowest point. A 20-minute walk across a working sheep farm (through stiles, leaving gates as found) reaches a cliff-edge signpost marking the distances to the equator (5,268 km north) and the South Pole. The trees at Slope Point are among the most photographed in New Zealand — coastal horopito and pittosporum permanently bent north-east by the prevailing wind, into distinctive combed shapes. The Southern Ocean on clear days: absolutely empty water from here to Antarctica.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Slope Point as quietly profound — the combination of the wind, the emptiness of the ocean, and the knowledge that the next land mass south is Antarctica creates a feeling that is specific and memorable. The wind-bent trees are called one of the most striking natural-art images in New Zealand. Reviewers note that Wellington weather feels calm by comparison.

pois.name: Fiordland Lodge Restaurant, Te Anau
pois.short: The finest kitchen in Fiordland — Fiordland Lodge's restaurant above Lake Te Anau, serving Southland venison, blue cod, and Central Otago pinot to a backdrop of the mountains.
pois.commentary: Fiordland Lodge is a luxury lodge on the ridge above Te Anau's northern end, with a restaurant open to non-lodge guests for dinner by reservation. The kitchen focuses on southern New Zealand provenance — Fiordland venison, Southland lamb, Foveaux Strait blue cod, and Southland river trout — matched to a Central Otago-dominant wine list. The view from the dining room over Lake Te Anau to the Murchison Mountains is, on a clear evening, extraordinary. It is the formal dining option for the Fiordland region and operates at a different level from the Te Anau township restaurants.

reviews.summary: Diners describe Fiordland Lodge's restaurant as the most satisfying meal in the region — the quality of the Southland produce, the wine list, and the view combine into an experience that regularly exceeds expectations for travellers not expecting fine dining in this location. The venison is consistently called out. Booking essential and often required weeks in advance.

pois.name: Sandy Brown's — Invercargill's Historic Oyster Bar
pois.short: Invercargill's local institution for Bluff oysters — a no-fuss oyster bar serving the Foveaux Strait catch in season, the way Southlanders have done for decades.
pois.commentary: Sandy Brown's is the local Invercargill institution for Bluff oyster consumption — a straight-forward café and oyster bar on Dee Street that serves fresh Foveaux Strait flat oysters in season (March–August) with minimal intervention: natural, kilpatrick, or mornay, with bread and butter. The approach is entirely opposed to the gastropub oyster aestheticisation found elsewhere — these are worker-town oysters served in a worker-town setting, and the quality of the ingredient makes this not only sufficient but exactly right. In season the bar is full of Invercargill locals, which is the only recommendation necessary.

reviews.summary: Visitors who find Sandy Brown's during the Bluff oyster season describe it as one of the best seafood experiences of their New Zealand trip — the simplicity of the oyster service and the quality of the Foveaux Strait flat oyster make the experience essentially unreproducible outside Southland. Reviewers consistently note that the local atmosphere is itself part of the value.

pois.name: Curio Bay Yellow-Eyed Penguin Colony
pois.short: A remote Catlins bay where the world's rarest penguin — the yellow-eyed penguin or hoiho — nests alongside a 180-million-year-old petrified fossil forest.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay is a remarkable dual-heritage site on the remote Catlins coast of Southland, combining a Jurassic-era petrified forest embedded in the rock platform with a nesting colony of yellow-eyed penguins, one of the world's rarest penguin species with only around 1600 breeding pairs remaining. The petrified stumps and logs date from 180 million years ago when this coastline was part of a vast Gondwana forest, and they are exposed at low tide in extraordinary detail. Yellow-eyed penguins come ashore in the late afternoon and early evening to feed their chicks in nest sites hidden in the coastal vegetation. Observation is from fixed hides and viewing platforms that keep visitor disturbance to a minimum during the sensitive nesting period.

reviews.summary: Curio Bay earns superlatives for the rare combination of Jurassic geology and critically endangered penguin watching in a single accessible location. Early evening visits during summer offer the best chance of penguin sightings.

pois.name: Catlins Coast Wildlife Drive
pois.short: A self-drive wildlife corridor along the Catlins coast of Southland encountering sea lions, Hectors dolphins, penguins, waterfalls, and one of New Zealand's most unspoiled coastlines.
pois.commentary: The Catlins stretch from Balclutha in South Otago to Fortrose in Southland and constitutes one of New Zealand's most ecologically rich and scenically diverse coastal environments, almost entirely bypassed by mainstream tourism. Nugget Point at the northern end supports a year-round New Zealand fur seal and sea lion colony at the base of its lighthouse headland, with royal spoonbills and spotted shags nesting on the sea stacks below. Porpoise Bay near Waikawa hosts the world's rarest dolphin — the Hectors dolphin — as a resident community visible from shore. Purakaunui Falls, Matai Falls, and McLean Falls are among the most beautiful cascades in the South Island, accessible via short forest walks from the main highway.

reviews.summary: The Catlins drive is increasingly recognised as one of New Zealand's greatest self-drive wildlife experiences, combining multiple species encounters with extraordinary coastal scenery and the satisfaction of genuine remoteness.

pois.name: The Catlins Scenic Route
pois.short: Winding coastal road through the Catlins linking waterfalls, wildlife, and petrified forests.
pois.commentary: The Southern Scenic Route through the Catlins is one of New Zealand's most underrated road trips, winding 180 kilometres along the rugged southeast coast. Key stops include Slope Point, Cathedral Caves, Purakaunui Falls, McLean Falls, Curio Bay, and Nugget Point lighthouse. Yellow-eyed penguins, fur seals, Hector's dolphins, and sea lions are commonly spotted.

reviews.summary: A wonderfully wild road trip through country most tourists miss. Take two days if possible.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary with extraordinary native bird encounters.
pois.commentary: Ulva Island in Paterson Inlet, Stewart Island/Rakiura, is one of New Zealand's most pristine open wildlife sanctuaries — a predator-free island of ancient podocarp forest where native birds regularly land on visitors. Walking tracks wind through towering rimu and totara forest. The island's bird density is among the highest in New Zealand. A 10-minute water taxi from Golden Bay Wharf provides access.

reviews.summary: The closest you'll get to pre-human New Zealand. Birds everywhere, fearless and curious.

pois.name: Tuatapere Hump Ridge Track
pois.short: 3-day Great Walk through coastal forest, alpine tops, and a spectacular historic viaduct.
pois.commentary: The Hump Ridge Track is New Zealand's newest Great Walk, a 3-day, 61-kilometre loop. The track combines dramatic coastal walking, podocarp forest, alpine tussock tops, and the extraordinary Percy Burn Viaduct — the largest remaining wooden rail viaduct in the world. Built and maintained by the Tuatapere community. Accommodation is in comfortable lodges.

reviews.summary: An outstanding new Great Walk with incredible variety. The viaduct is jaw-dropping.

pois.name: Riverton / Aparima
pois.short: One of NZ's oldest settlements with excellent cafes and quirky heritage museum.
pois.commentary: Riverton (Aparima) is one of New Zealand's oldest European settlements, established as a whaling and sealing base in the early 1800s. Today it's a charming coastal village known for its excellent cafes — particularly Mrs Clark's on the main street — and the outstanding Te Hikoi Southern Journey museum.
