The museum tells the story of southern New Zealand's human history from the earliest Polynesian explorers through the whaling era to modern times, and is consistently rated one of the best small museums in the country. The town sits at the mouth of the Aparima River with views across Foveaux Strait to Stewart Island. Colac Bay, a few kilometres west, is a popular local beach. Riverton's relaxed pace and creative community make it an appealing stop on the Southern Scenic Route.

reviews.summary: A delightful small town with surprisingly excellent cafes and museum. Mrs Clark's coffee is legendary. Te Hikoi museum is outstanding.

pois.name: Bill Richardson Transport World / Invercargill
pois.short: Award-winning automotive museum with Southern Hemisphere's largest private vehicle collection.
pois.commentary: Bill Richardson Transport World in Invercargill houses the Southern Hemisphere's largest private collection of vintage and classic vehicles, displayed across a series of themed exhibition spaces designed by acclaimed museum designers. The collection includes trucks, cars, motorcycles, and petroliana spanning over a century of motoring history. The presentation is world-class — far beyond a typical vehicle museum — with atmospheric lighting, period settings, and interactive elements. The adjacent Classic Motorcycle Mecca focuses on two-wheeled history with over 300 machines. The museum has won multiple New Zealand and international tourism awards. It anchors a growing cultural precinct in Invercargill that includes E Hayes hardware store (home of Burt Munro's famous Indian motorcycle) and the Invercargill Brewery.

reviews.summary: World-class museum presentation that surprises everyone. Even non-car-enthusiasts are impressed. Multiple award winner.

pois.name: Doubtful Sound / Patea
pois.short: Remote fiord three times longer than Milford Sound, famous for its profound silence and dolphins.
pois.commentary: Doubtful Sound is Fiordland's largest and most remote fiord — three times longer and ten times larger than Milford Sound, yet receiving a fraction of the visitors. Reaching Doubtful Sound requires a boat across Lake Manapouri and a bus over Wilmot Pass, making the journey itself part of the adventure. The fiord's defining quality is its profound silence — in the inner reaches, the only sounds are birdsong and the occasional splash of a bottlenose dolphin. A resident pod of around 60 bottlenose dolphins lives in the fiord, along with fur seals and Fiordland crested penguins. Overnight cruises allow the full experience of the fiord's moods.

reviews.summary: Magnificently remote and peaceful. The silence is almost tangible. Overnight cruises are the way to experience it.

pois.name: McLean Falls & Catlins Coastal Walk
pois.short: Spectacular 22m waterfall in ancient podocarp forest in the Catlins.
pois.commentary: McLean Falls is one of the Catlins' most impressive waterfalls — a 22-metre cascade dropping through a slot in ancient podocarp forest. The walk to the falls (40 minutes return) passes through magnificent bush with towering rimu and rata trees, ferns, and abundant birdlife. The falls themselves drop into a dark pool surrounded by moss-covered rocks and overhanging trees. The Catlins coast, stretching between Invercargill and Balclutha, is one of New Zealand's least-visited stretches of coastline despite its wealth of natural attractions — waterfalls, sea caves, penguin colonies, and fossil forests. McLean Falls is one of the area's highlights.

reviews.summary: Beautiful waterfall in magnificent bush setting. The walk is easy and atmospheric. The whole Catlins coast is worth exploring.

pois.name: Lake Marian Track
pois.short: Stunning glacier lake hidden in the mountains of Fiordland — a challenging but spectacular day walk.
pois.commentary: The Lake Marian Track is a 3-hour return hike that leads through lush Fiordland beech forest before revealing a stunning glacier-carved lake surrounded by towering peaks. The final section is steep and can be muddy, but the reward — an alpine lake reflecting snow-capped mountains — is breathtaking. The lake sits in a classic glacial cirque, with waterfalls cascading down the rock walls. On calm days, the reflections are mirror-perfect. The track starts from the Hollyford Road, between Te Anau and Milford Sound. It's challenging enough to filter out casual visitors, keeping the lake peaceful. In winter, snow adds drama. This is one of Fiordland's most spectacular day walks.

reviews.summary: Stunning glacier lake that feels like a major discovery. The final reveal is breathtaking. Steep but worth every step.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara — Bird Sanctuary
pois.short: Predator-free island in Paterson Inlet with extraordinary native birdlife at arm's length.
pois.commentary: Ulva Island (Te Wharawhara) is a predator-free bird sanctuary in Paterson Inlet, Stewart Island, where native birds are so unafraid of humans that they approach within arm's reach. The island's ancient podocarp forest hosts kākā, saddleback, Stewart Island robin, rifleman, weka, and yellowhead in extraordinary densities. Guided walks with local naturalists reveal birds that are virtually impossible to see this closely anywhere else. The island is a short water-taxi ride from Golden Bay wharf on Stewart Island. Walking tracks are well maintained and flat. For birdwatchers, Ulva Island is one of the ultimate New Zealand experiences — the intimacy of the bird encounters is unmatched.

reviews.summary: The best birdwatching experience in New Zealand. Birds approach to within arm's reach. Guided walks are outstanding. Unmissable.

pois.name: Slope Point — Southernmost Mainland Point
pois.short: The southernmost point of the South Island — wind-battered trees and wild coastal scenery.
pois.commentary: Slope Point is the southernmost point of the South Island of New Zealand, marked by a signpost and the famous wind-sculpted trees bent almost horizontal by the relentless Antarctic winds. A 20-minute walk across farmland from the car park reaches the headland, where views extend south toward the sub-Antarctic. The wind here can be ferocious — the permanently bent macrocarpa trees are testament to its power. There's something compelling about standing at geographical extremes, and Slope Point delivers a genuine sense of remoteness and exposure to the elements. The surrounding Catlins coastline is dramatic and sparsely populated. The best light for photography is late afternoon.

reviews.summary: A compelling geographical extreme with dramatic wind-bent trees. The sense of remoteness is powerful. Quick visit but very memorable.

pois.name: Key Summit Track — Fiordland
pois.short: Alpine wetland walk with panoramic views of three major Fiordland valleys — on the Routeburn Track.
pois.commentary: The Key Summit Track is a 3-hour return walk that branches off the Routeburn Track to reach an alpine wetland with panoramic views of three major Fiordland valleys — the Hollyford, Eglinton, and Greenstone. The summit boardwalk traverses a unique alpine bog ecosystem with cushion plants, sundews, and tiny alpine species. The 360-degree mountain views from the summit are extraordinary. The track starts from The Divide on the Milford Road, making it accessible as a day walk for anyone driving between Te Anau and Milford Sound. The relatively moderate gradient (compared to other Fiordland tracks) makes it manageable for reasonably fit walkers. On clear days, it's one of Fiordland's most rewarding short walks.

reviews.summary: Outstanding views from an accessible alpine walk. The alpine bog ecosystem is unique. Perfect stop on the Milford Road.

pois.name: McLean Falls / Te Tangi o Tawhirimatea
pois.short: 22m waterfall cascading through native bush in the heart of the Catlins.
pois.commentary: McLean Falls is one of the Catlins' most beautiful waterfalls, a 22-metre cascade tumbling over a rocky ledge into a bush-fringed pool. The falls are reached by a well-formed 40-minute return track through dense podocarp and beech forest typical of the Catlins region. The forest is home to native birds including kākā, kererū, and bellbirds, and the damp bush is rich with ferns, mosses, and lichens. The waterfall itself is dramatic, with the water splitting into multiple streams as it drops over the cliff face. A viewing platform at the base provides excellent photo opportunities. Located deep in the Catlins, it rewards visitors who take the time to explore this remote coastal region. Free access; the track can be muddy after rain.

reviews.summary: The waterfall is stunning and the bush walk is beautiful. The Catlins setting adds to the remote, wild atmosphere. The track can be muddy but is well-maintained.

pois.name: Teapot Land, Owaka
pois.short: A whimsical garden in the Catlins adorned with over 1,200 teapots — delightfully eccentric roadside art.
pois.commentary: Teapot Land in Owaka is exactly what it sounds like — a garden festooned with over 1,200 teapots of every size, shape, colour and vintage, dangling from trees, perched on fences, nestled among flowers and arranged in elaborate displays. It started as a small collection and grew into a full-blown installation that now draws visitors from around the world. The owner maintains it with obvious love and humour, and entry is free (donations appreciated). It's the kind of gloriously eccentric Kiwi folk art that you can only find by leaving the main highways. Allow 20 minutes and bring a camera.

reviews.summary: A delightful and quirky surprise. Photography opportunities are endless. The owner's dedication is impressive. A brief but memorable stop in the Catlins.

pois.name: Monkey Island / Te Puka o Takitimu Walk
pois.short: A tiny island accessible on foot at low tide, with stairs to the summit offering spectacular views over Te Waewae Bay and Fiordland.
pois.commentary: Monkey Island is a small rocky islet connected to the shore at low tide near Orepuki. A stairway carved into the rock leads to the summit, where views stretch across Te Waewae Bay to the snow-capped mountains of Fiordland. The name has nothing to do with primates — it likely derives from a corruption of a Māori name. The island is best visited at low tide when you can walk across the sand causeway; check tide tables before setting out. The surrounding beach is wild and windswept, and the sense of standing at the edge of the world is palpable.

reviews.summary: The views from the summit are breathtaking. Check tide times — you can get stranded. The stairs are steep but manageable. A wonderful off-the-beaten-track stop in western Southland.

pois.name: Colac Bay / Ōraka Aparima Surfing
pois.short: An uncrowded surf beach in southern Southland with consistent breaks, wide sands, and sunsets over Foveaux Strait.
pois.commentary: Colac Bay is tucked away south of Riverton, offering a quieter, more authentic coastal experience than the more famous surf spots further north. The beach receives consistent swells from the Southern Ocean, producing reliable waves that suit intermediate surfers. The wide, sandy beach is rarely crowded — on a good day you might share the lineup with half a dozen locals. The sunsets here are extraordinary, with the sky turning gold and crimson over Foveaux Strait and Stewart Island silhouetted on the horizon. The small settlement has a holiday-park feel and a genuinely friendly local community.

reviews.summary: Great surf in uncrowded conditions. The sunsets are legendary. Water is cold — a good wetsuit is essential. The beach is wide and beautiful. A peaceful escape from tourist routes.

pois.name: Two Wee Bookshops & The Snug, Manapōuri
pois.short: Two of Aotearoa's smallest bookshops, tucked away in Manapōuri — the tiniest for children's books, the larger for southern stories.
pois.commentary: Two Wee Bookshops are exactly that — two diminutive bookshops in Manapōuri, each barely bigger than a garden shed. The smaller focuses entirely on children's books, while the larger stocks titles about the deep south, Fiordland natural history, and New Zealand writing. The attached Snug café serves excellent coffee. Run by a couple with deep roots in the Fiordland community, the shops are a labour of love that punches well above their square footage. It's the perfect stop before or after a lake cruise, and the kind of place where a quick browse turns into an hour of discovery.

reviews.summary: Utterly charming and surprisingly well-stocked. The owners are warm and knowledgeable. The coffee is excellent. Don't skip it on your way to or from Doubtful Sound.

pois.name: Switzers Museum, Waikaia
pois.short: A community museum in Waikaia preserving the stories and artefacts of Southland's gold-mining era and rural heritage.
pois.commentary: Switzers Museum in the small eastern Southland town of Waikaia is one of those volunteer-run gems that rewards the curious traveller. The collection spans the region's gold-mining heritage, farming history and community life, with artefacts ranging from mining equipment to household objects that tell the story of pioneer life in inland Southland. The museum is named after the Swiss settlers who were among the first to mine gold in the area. Volunteers are usually on hand to add stories that no label could capture. It's a genuine slice of provincial New Zealand heritage.

reviews.summary: A charming small-town museum with passionate volunteers. The gold-mining exhibits are interesting. Limited opening hours — check ahead. Worth combining with the drive through rural Southland.

pois.name: Fiordland Crested Penguin / Tawaki Viewing
pois.short: See the rare tawaki (Fiordland crested penguin) — one of the world's rarest penguin species — on the remote southwest coast.
pois.commentary: The tawaki or Fiordland crested penguin is the third rarest penguin species on earth, breeding only on the remote southwest coast of the South Island and a few offshore islands. Breeding pairs arrive from July and can be seen at several coastal sites, with Monro Beach near Lake Moeraki being the most accessible viewing location. The penguins nest in coastal rainforest and can be spotted crossing the beach at dawn and dusk during the breeding season (July-November). Approach quietly, stay low, and maintain at least 10 metres distance. Seeing these striking, yellow-crested birds in their wild rainforest habitat is one of New Zealand's most exclusive wildlife encounters.

reviews.summary: A rare and special wildlife experience. Sightings are seasonal and not guaranteed. Dawn and dusk visits are essential. The remote location adds to the magic. Patience and quiet are rewarded.

pois.name: Wairaurahiri Jet, Tuatapere
pois.short: A jet-boat adventure from Tuatapere into the wilderness rivers at the edge of Fiordland — deep into pristine rainforest.
pois.commentary: The Wairaurahiri Jet takes visitors from Tuatapere into the roadless wilderness at the southern edge of Fiordland National Park, navigating rivers that cut through virgin podocarp rainforest beneath mountains. The journey passes through gorges, across rapids, and into country that is virtually unchanged since before human arrival. Your driver-guide shares natural history and stories of the southern pioneers who attempted to settle this impossibly remote coast. The experience is raw and exhilarating — genuine wilderness access without the multi-day tramp. The return by helicopter (on some tours) adds a dramatic aerial perspective.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wilderness jet-boat experience. The remoteness is genuine. Guides are passionate about the environment. Weather-dependent — be flexible. One of the most thrilling activities in Southland.

pois.name: Stewart Island Wild Kiwi Spotting Tour
pois.short: The only place in New Zealand where kiwi are regularly seen in the wild during daylight — a guided encounter on Stewart Island's beaches.
pois.commentary: Stewart Island (Rakiura) is home to an estimated 13,000 Southern Tokoeka kiwi — more kiwi than people. Unlike elsewhere in New Zealand, these kiwi sometimes forage on the beaches during daylight, especially in autumn and winter, making Stewart Island the best place in the country for a wild kiwi encounter. Guided evening tours take small groups by boat to remote beaches at dusk, waiting quietly for the birds to emerge from the bush. Sighting rates are high but not guaranteed — patience is essential. Seeing a kiwi foraging on a wild beach, with only the sound of the sea and the forest, is one of New Zealand's most profound wildlife experiences.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience. Sighting rates are good but not guaranteed. The guides are passionate and knowledgeable. The remote setting adds magic. Best in autumn/winter. Dress warmly.

pois.name: Curio Bay Petrified Forest & Beach Walk
pois.short: Walk across a 180-million-year-old petrified forest exposed on a wave-cut platform — one of the world's finest examples of Jurassic fossils.
pois.commentary: Curio Bay's petrified forest is a geological marvel — the fossilised stumps, logs and foliage of a 180-million-year-old Jurassic forest, exposed on a wave-cut platform at the southeastern tip of the South Island. At low tide, the tree stumps and fallen trunks are clearly visible in the rock, some still showing growth rings and bark patterns. The interpretive panels explain the geological processes that preserved the forest and the ancient environment it reveals. In the evening, yellow-eyed penguins often come ashore on the adjacent beach, creating an improbable combination of Jurassic geology and living wildlife. Visit at low tide for best fossil viewing.

reviews.summary: A remarkable geological site. The fossils are clearly visible at low tide. The penguin viewing is a bonus. Interpretation panels are excellent. Some visitors find it modest compared to expectations — manage accordingly.

pois.name: E Hayes & Sons / Burt Munro Heritage
pois.short: A hardware store housing a world-famous motorcycle collection — including Burt Munro's original 'World's Fastest Indian' record-breaking bike.
pois.commentary: E Hayes & Sons is ostensibly a hardware store in central Invercargill, but it houses one of the most significant motorcycle collections in the Southern Hemisphere. The centrepiece is Burt Munro's original Indian Scout — the motorcycle he rode to a world speed record at Bonneville Salt Flats in 1967, immortalised in the film The World's Fastest Indian starring Anthony Hopkins. The bike sits alongside other vintage and racing motorcycles, all within a working hardware shop that's been trading since 1932. The combination of retail hardware, world-class motorcycles and genuine Southland hospitality is uniquely New Zealand. Free to view.

reviews.summary: A unique and charming experience. Burt Munro's bike is genuinely thrilling to see. The hardware store setting is part of the appeal. Free and friendly. A must-visit in Invercargill.

pois.name: Cosy Nook / Mullet Bay
pois.short: Whimsical fishing hamlet nestled among giant granite boulders on the southern coast.
pois.commentary: Cosy Nook is a tiny collection of colourful fishing shacks tucked between enormous granite boulders on Southland's wild coast. The surreal landscape of house-sized rocks and weathered huts feels otherworldly — one of New Zealand's most photogenic hidden gems.

reviews.summary: Utterly charming and quirky — a photographer's dream.

pois.name: Ulva Island / Te Wharawhara — Stewart Island
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary with some of New Zealand's rarest birds.
pois.commentary: Ulva Island is a pristine predator-free bird sanctuary in Paterson Inlet, Stewart Island. The short water taxi from Golden Bay delivers you to an island where South Island saddleback, yellowhead, and Stewart Island robin forage fearlessly at your feet — world-class birdwatching.

reviews.summary: Best birdwatching experience possible — the birds are incredibly tame.

pois.name: Gemstone Beach — Ōrepuki
pois.short: Beach renowned for semi-precious stones, fossils, and petrified wood.
pois.commentary: Gemstone Beach near Ōrepuki is famous for its semi-precious stones washed down from the mountains over millennia. Beachcombers find jasper, garnet, quartz, and occasionally petrified wood among the pebbles — a natural treasure hunt on a wild southern coastline.

reviews.summary: Wonderful beachcombing — we found several beautiful stones.
